one_liner: Every Pedigree ad dog is a real shelter dog near you.
search_doc:
Every Pedigree ad dog is a real shelter dog near you.
Mechanisms: ai-powered-personalization, media-as-product, purpose-driven-media, geolocation-targeting
Idea: A bespoke AI system transforms a single image of a shelter dog into studio-quality photography, then maps it to a CGI rig so the photo-real dog can be posed for any Pedigree creative, making every digital ad an ad for a real, nearby adoptable dog.
Insight: Shelter dogs are often overlooked because their photos look unprofessional compared to the polished imagery used in brand advertising.

extracted.tagline: Turning every PEDIGREE ad into an ad for a shelter dog.
extracted.context: Every Pedigree ad features a dog. The brand wondered what if that dog could be a real adoptable shelter dog.
extracted.idea: A bespoke AI system transforms a single image of a shelter dog into studio-quality photography, then maps it to a CGI rig so the photo-real dog can be posed for any Pedigree creative, making every digital ad an ad for a real, nearby adoptable dog.
execution: OOH digital billboards featuring individual shelter dogs (e.g. 'Meet Candy', 'Or Colin')
Geolocation-targeted placements near shelters where each dog lives
Ads instantly removed from rotation once a dog is adopted
AI 'glow up' transformation of shelter photos into studio-quality images
CGI rig mapping allowing dogs to be reposed for any creative
Mobile/app experience with QR code ('Scan me / Meet me') to meet the dog (e.g. 'Hi! I'm Tiny.')
Series of yellow Pedigree poster ads each featuring a different shelter dog
metrics: 50% of dogs adopted in first 2 weeks

inferred.insight: Shelter dogs are often overlooked because their photos look unprofessional compared to the polished imagery used in brand advertising.
inferred.one_liner: Every Pedigree ad dog is a real shelter dog near you.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Reframed Pedigree's entire digital media spend as a direct adoption engine, turning every brand impression into a chance to rehome a local shelter dog and driving a six-fold increase in shelter site visits.

visual.style_description: Clean, editorial layout with a large hero CGI/photo-real dog dominating the center against a soft neutral background, flanked by serif headline typography and a right-side gallery of campaign executions. Confident, gallery-style composition with generous whitespace.

idea.rationale: Turning every Pedigree dog ad into a real, nearby, adoptable shelter dog is a category-redefining reframe of media itself as the product — the dog in the ad IS the dog you can adopt. It lands instantly on a billboard with just a name ('Meet Candy') and a face, making it natively outdoor rather than a film compressed into a poster.

strategy.rationale: Pedigree is Mars's global dog-food brand whose long-running 'Feed the Good' / 'Dogs Rule' mission has anchored shelter-adoption advocacy for two decades, so converting paid media into an adoption engine is exact mission-fit, not borrowed purpose. The insight — shelter dogs are overlooked because their photos look amateur next to polished brand imagery — is a plain, observable cultural truth that the AI 'glow up' directly attacks; the only soft spot is that the board cites the insight as self-evident rather than backing it with adoption-rate data.

execution.rationale: Craft is high: the AI-to-CGI rig genuinely lifts shaky shelter snapshots to studio-grade Pedigree-yellow poster quality, and the system is wired to geolocated DOOH near each dog's shelter with auto-removal on adoption and a QR 'Scan me / Meet me' handoff to a mobile profile. The orchestration — single hero image → AI restoration → CGI repose → localised DOOH → mobile adoption funnel — uses the media channel itself as the conversion layer, which is the right channel for a glanceable, location-bound call to action.

impact.rationale: The headline behaviour-change number — 50% of featured dogs adopted within two weeks — is the right business outcome for an adoption campaign, though the cohort size isn't disclosed on the board, so '50% of how many dogs?' remains open; the 6x lift in shelter site visits is a credible directional proxy. The '100% purpose-led media investment by 2026' is a brand commitment rather than a result, but it signals durable cultural footprint beyond the campaign window.

why_it_won.verdict: Grand Prix because it doesn't just advertise adoption — it rebuilds the brand's entire media buy into an adoption product, with each impression tied to a specific, local, adoptable dog that disappears the moment she finds a home.

one_liner: The billboard becomes a sunshine map.
search_doc:
The billboard becomes a sunshine map.
Mechanisms: data-as-content, real-time-geolocation, context-aware-ooh, category-reframe
Idea: A billboard that tracks every sunny spot in the city in real-time, encouraging people to enjoy a Magnum during winter sunshine moments.
Insight: People still enjoy little moments of sunshine during winter, which can be opportunities to have an ice cream.

full_record.file_path: 2024/OUTDOOR/GRAND PRIX/Find your Summer - Magnum (Lola Mullenlowe, Madrid).jpeg

extracted.tagline: Find your summer
extracted.context: In the UK, ice cream volume sales drop by more than half in the low season — from 140M litres to only 60M.
extracted.insight: People still enjoy little moments of sunshine during winter, which can be opportunities to have an ice cream.
extracted.idea: A billboard that tracks every sunny spot in the city in real-time, encouraging people to enjoy a Magnum during winter sunshine moments.
execution: Digital billboards displaying exact sunny spot locations and times
Real-time city sun-tracking 3D map
Beacons on billboards sending geo-located push notifications
App directing people to the nearest store with a Magnum discount
OOH placements across city streets and bus shelters

inferred.one_liner: The billboard becomes a sunshine map.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Reframed Magnum as a winter indulgence by turning fleeting sunny moments into purchase triggers, lifting cold-season sales well above the seasonal norm and earning broad international press coverage.

visual.style_description: Moody black-and-white street photography centered on a lone figure standing in a shaft of sunlight, with elegant serif typography and a clean case-study grid layout around the hero image.

idea.rationale: Turning a billboard into a real-time sunshine map is a sharp category reframe — it transforms outdoor media from a static ad into a live utility that creates the consumption moment. The mnemonic is immediate and native to OOH: a billboard that literally points you to the sun and an ice cream nearby. It also flips Magnum's seasonal problem (winter slump) into a creative mechanism rather than ignoring it.

strategy.rationale: Magnum is a globally known Unilever premium ice-cream brand whose mission centers on adult indulgence and 'pleasure' — extending those pleasure moments into winter is fully coherent with brand DNA. The context is data-backed and specific: UK ice cream volume drops from 140M to 60M litres in low season, a real category problem, not a manufactured one. The insight — that fleeting winter sunshine still triggers small pleasure moments — is a credible cultural truth in a notoriously grey UK climate.

execution.rationale: Means fit objective tightly: digital billboards rendering a 3D sun-tracking city map, beacons pushing geo-located notifications when a sunny spot opens nearby, and an app routing users to the closest store with a Magnum discount — a complete trigger-to-purchase loop happening in OOH space. Placement on street-level boards and bus shelters puts the prompt exactly where commuters encounter sunshine. The black-and-white case craft is polished and the multi-layer orchestration (billboard → notification → app → store) is coherent rather than bolted on.

impact.rationale: The headline business result — +66.3% sales vs. average winter season — is the right metric for the stated problem (winter category collapse), not a surrogate like impressions, though '+66.3% of what absolute baseline' isn't disclosed on the board. The press list is broad (Times, Guardian, Telegraph, AdAge, Fast Company, Campaign) but should be read as claimed pickup; even discounted, the combination of a category-relevant sales lift and tier-1 trade/consumer press coverage is substantial. No long-tail behaviour-change data, but for a seasonal commercial brief the business proof is on-target.

why_it_won.verdict: It won the Grand Prix because it solved a real, quantified category problem — winter ice-cream collapse — with an idea that is unmistakably native to outdoor: the billboard itself becomes the demand-generation engine, tracking sunshine in real time and converting it into sales.

one_liner: The sponsorship IS the absence of sponsorship.
search_doc:
The sponsorship IS the absence of sponsorship.
Mechanisms: category-convention-subversion, absence-as-presence, cultural-symbol-restoration, anti-branding
Idea: Consul sponsored Juventus (Brazil) but bought up every logo space on the jersey and left it empty — giving fans a clean kit with no visual interferences, including their own logo.
Insight: Fans don't want more sponsor logos on their beloved jerseys — they want fewer. A sponsor that respects the jersey wins more love than one that brands it.

extracted.tagline: The kind of sponsorship fans actually want
extracted.context: Brazilians care deeply about football jerseys. In recent years, clubs have added up to 25 sponsor logos to their kits to fend off economic problems, ruining iconic designs and angering fans.
extracted.idea: Consul sponsored Juventus (Brazil) but bought up every logo space on the jersey and left it empty — giving fans a clean kit with no visual interferences, including their own logo.
execution: Bought all sponsor spaces on Juventus club jersey
Removed every logo (including Consul's own) for a clean jersey
Worn across 20 official games in the entire 2024 season
In-stadium and broadcast presence during matches
press_coverage: "Clean Sponsorship proves there's more to sponsorship than meets the eye" - Lance!
"Consul logo is nowhere to be seen and yet it is now on everyone's mind" - Placar
"Not showing their (and any) logo made Consul win the hearts of every football fan" - Estadão

inferred.insight: Fans don't want more sponsor logos on their beloved jerseys — they want fewer. A sponsor that respects the jersey wins more love than one that brands it.
inferred.one_liner: The sponsorship IS the absence of sponsorship.
inferred.qualitative_summary: By buying every logo slot on a beloved Brazilian club's jersey and leaving it blank, Consul restored an iconic kit to its fans and reframed sponsorship as an act of respect rather than clutter, earning massive cultural goodwill and media attention without ever showing its logo.

visual.style_description: Editorial case-study layout with a clear before/after centerpiece of two jerseys, framed by structured columns of body copy, pull quotes, match photography grid, and metrics. Clean typographic hierarchy with a spray-paint headline treatment evoking the 'clean' concept.

idea.rationale: Buying every sponsor slot on a football jersey — including your own — to deliver a logo-free kit is a clean, one-glance subversion of the entire sponsorship category. It works as an outdoor idea because the jersey itself becomes the medium, legible across stadium, broadcast and press without explanation. The mnemonic is the absence — a rare case where 'no logo' is the logo.

strategy.rationale: Consul is a Brazilian home-appliance brand (Whirlpool-owned), best known domestically for washing machines — its mission ladders cleanly to 'clean,' making the wordplay between a clean wash and a clean jersey coherent rather than opportunistic. The context is verifiable: Brazilian clubs have piled on sponsors (the board cites up to 25 logos per kit) as a documented response to club financial distress, and fan frustration with cluttered jerseys is a real cultural truth in Brazilian football. The insight — fans want fewer logos, not more — is intuitive but not backed on the board by a specific study, which is the only soft spot.

execution.rationale: The media choice is the idea: a real Série B club (Juventus SP) jersey worn across all 20 official 2024-season matches, guaranteeing in-stadium and broadcast presence to football audiences in their native habitat. The orchestration runs from the season-long jersey deployment into a sports-press PR loop (Lance!, Placar, Estadão, ESPN, Reuters, Marca, L'Equipe) where the absence of a logo becomes the headline — a self-propelling amplification layer. Craft is high: the case board uses a clean editorial layout and a before/after jersey comparison that lands the concept in a glance.

impact.rationale: Claimed results are strong and span surrogate and business metrics: +2,300% brand mentions, +400% brand love, 28M earned media impressions, and — crucially — a +12% sales lift, which is the right business outcome for an appliance brand and the most load-bearing number here. The percentage gains lack disclosed baselines (2,300% of what monthly volume?), and the press list and earned-media figure are entrant claims rather than independently verified, so the surrogate metrics should be read with caution. Still, the cultural footprint in Brazilian football media and the named sales movement together justify a high but not maximal impact read.

why_it_won.verdict: It won Gold because the brand paid for sponsorship space and then refused to use it — a single, category-redefining gesture that turned a washing-machine brand into the most-loved sponsor in Brazilian football by literally disappearing.

one_liner: Ohtani's foul ball becomes the new Coors Light can.
search_doc:
Ohtani's foul ball becomes the new Coors Light can.
Mechanisms: reactive-marketing, flaw-as-feature, unofficial-sponsorship, real-world-moment-to-product
Idea: Instead of fixing the broken ad, Coors Light turned the flaw into an unofficial sports sponsorship by replicating it as a special-edition can and adding the same black square to existing ads.
Insight: In sports marketing, official sponsorships cost fortunes — but a single unscripted moment from a superstar can be worth more than any paid deal.

full_record.file_path: 2024/OUTDOOR/GOLD/Coors Lights Out, Coors Light - Rethink, Toronto).jpeg

extracted.tagline: Coors Lights Out: How a destroyed Coors Light ad turned into a new can design and unofficial sports sponsorship.
extracted.context: Shohei Ohtani broke a Coors Light ad with a foul ball, leaving an embarrassing black square flaw on the billboard.
extracted.idea: Instead of fixing the broken ad, Coors Light turned the flaw into an unofficial sports sponsorship by replicating it as a special-edition can and adding the same black square to existing ads.
execution: Shohei Ohtani broke the ad, adding a black square to the can imagery
Replicated the moment with a special edition can within 48 hours
Broke their own existing ads and screens inside Angel Stadium to match
Campaign spread virally across social media globally
Fans created their own cans and merch
Brought Coors Light cans to Japan for the first time ever
metrics: Trended higher than the Official Beer of MLB
press_coverage: Creative Bloq: "A lesson in clever reactive marketing."

inferred.insight: In sports marketing, official sponsorships cost fortunes — but a single unscripted moment from a superstar can be worth more than any paid deal.
inferred.one_liner: Ohtani's foul ball becomes the new Coors Light can.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Coors Light hijacked a Shohei Ohtani moment to create an unofficial baseball sponsorship without spending on endorsements, becoming baseball memorabilia and launching the brand into Japan for the first time.

visual.style_description: Photo-driven sports composition with a hero shot of the dented can held against a blurred stadium backdrop, flanked by Ohtani mid-swing and a grid of supporting case images. Dense editorial case-study layout with bold sans-serif headlines and pull quotes.

idea.rationale: Turning a literal flaw — a black square punched into a billboard by Ohtani's foul ball — into a special-edition can design is a textbook 'flaw-as-feature' move that reads in a glance and is instantly memorable. It's native to outdoor because the outdoor accident IS the idea; the can simply codifies it. Reactive at the speed culture demands, and visually mnemonic enough to spawn fan-made replicas.

strategy.rationale: Coors Light is a mainstream American light-beer brand whose mission centres on refreshment tied to sports and 'chill' moments — being in-stadium and adjacent to baseball culture is on-mission. The idea is coherent: rather than buy MLB official-sponsor status (held by a competitor), the brand exploited a real, verifiable on-field moment (Ohtani's foul ball into their Angel Stadium billboard, widely covered) as an unofficial sponsorship hook. The insight that an unscripted superstar moment can outperform paid endorsement is intuitive rather than data-sourced, but the contextual trigger is unambiguously real.

execution.rationale: Phasing was tight and opportunistic: the accidental billboard damage was left intact, a special-edition can replicating the dent shipped within 48 hours, and in-stadium screens and existing ads were retro-broken to match — creating a coherent multi-surface system. Channels fit the target (in-venue billboards and screens for baseball fans, social for virality, physical product as collectible), and amplification layered organically through press (LA Times, AdAge), fan-made merch, and a first-ever Japan distribution riding Ohtani's home-market gravity. Craft on the hero can and OOH retrofits is clean and consistent.

impact.rationale: The board claims the special-edition can sold out in under 24 hours (base undisclosed — sold-out of how many units?), 100% positive sentiment (methodology unstated), and that Coors Light trended above the Official Beer of MLB (window and measure unspecified) — all credible given the cultural moment but soft as proof. The strongest, verifiable outcomes are the $0 endorsement cost against a competitor's paid MLB deal and the first-ever launch of Coors Light cans in Japan, which is a tangible distribution result disproportionate to spend. Cultural footprint (fan-made cans, global pickup) is real even if individual metrics are unbased.

why_it_won.verdict: It wins Gold because Coors Light let an accident become the product — a single reactive gesture that hijacked baseball's biggest star, cost nothing in sponsorship, and got the brand into Japan for the first time ever.

one_liner: The voice of the NYC subway is transgender.
search_doc:
The voice of the NYC subway is transgender.
Mechanisms: celebrity-as-prop, ambient-media-hijack, cultural-symbol-subversion, purpose-driven-platform
Idea: Bernie Wagenblast, the famous announcer voice of the NYC subway, recently came out as a transgender woman — her voice now guides New Yorkers to trans understanding and acceptance.
Insight: Visibility is the most effective tool for change. People who personally know a transgender person report nearly double the rate of acceptance.

full_record.file_path: 2024/OUTDOOR/GOLD/In Transit, Callen-Lorde, Metropolitan Transportation Authority, NYC LGBT Historic Sites Pro (AREA 23).jpeg

extracted.tagline: The iconic voice of the NYC subway is now guiding new yorkers to transgender understanding and acceptance
extracted.context: Transgender people are under attack, facing anti-trans legislation, higher rates of suicide and physical violence, and media misrepresentation that reinforces harmful stereotypes.
extracted.insight: Visibility is the most effective tool for change. People who personally know a transgender person report nearly double the rate of acceptance.
extracted.idea: Bernie Wagenblast, the famous announcer voice of the NYC subway, recently came out as a transgender woman — her voice now guides New Yorkers to trans understanding and acceptance.
execution: New recording: a new MTA subway announcement in Bernie's voice with a personal message promoting trans awareness
Subway audio announcements broadcast during Transgender Day of Visibility week
Subway station takeover at Christopher Street station with traditional and digital OOH
In Transit podcast series hosted by Bernie, with episodes the length of a typical subway ride
T Line subway map highlighting historical landmarks significant to NYC's transgender community
press_coverage: "I hope, by sharing my story, folks will be reminded that transgender people are a part of their everyday life each time they hear me" – Bernie Wagenblast

inferred.one_liner: The voice of the NYC subway is transgender.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Turned a daily, unnoticed piece of NYC infrastructure into a powerful act of trans visibility, reframing millions of commutes as moments of acceptance and reminding New Yorkers that transgender people are already part of their everyday lives.

visual.style_description: Cinematic portrait of Bernie Wagenblast on a subway platform, overlaid with pink, white, and blue sound waves echoing the trans flag, and a horizontal infographic timeline of campaign executions below. Polished editorial composition with confident typography hierarchy.

idea.rationale: Revealing that the famously ubiquitous voice of the NYC subway is a transgender woman is a perfect ambient-media hijack — it reframes an infrastructure New Yorkers hear thousands of times into a daily act of visibility. The mnemonic is one-line clear ('the voice of the subway is trans') and lands without explanatory copy, which is precisely what Outdoor rewards.

strategy.rationale: WHO: The MTA is New York's public transit authority — a civic operator whose mission includes serving and reflecting all New Yorkers, making a visibility platform mission-coherent rather than opportunistic. The idea fits because Bernie Wagenblast is literally the MTA's voice, so the brand isn't borrowing a cause, it's revealing a truth already inside its own infrastructure. The insight — that personal acquaintance with a trans person nearly doubles acceptance rates — is a known contact-hypothesis finding, though the board cites it without a specific source attribution.

execution.rationale: The media-target fit is ideal: subway audio announcements during Transgender Day of Visibility week, a Christopher Street station takeover (the symbolic heart of LGBTQ+ NYC near Stonewall), OOH, a podcast timed to subway-ride length, and a T Line map of trans landmarks — each channel uses the MTA's own assets natively. Orchestration layers the audio (ambient reveal) with station OOH (depth) and podcast/map (sustain), and craft is editorial-grade with trans-flag sound waves anchoring the visual system.

impact.rationale: The board provides no hard metrics — no impressions, listener numbers, sentiment shifts, or press tallies — only a Bernie quote, so cultural impact must be inferred from the inherent scale of the NYC subway (millions of daily riders exposed to the announcement during TDOV week). The cultural footprint is real and the story was widely picked up in trade and mainstream press at the time, but on the board itself the results column is essentially a claim of reach-by-association rather than verified outcome data.

why_it_won.verdict: It won Gold because the single creative act — outing the subway's own voice as trans — turned the city's most repeated piece of audio infrastructure into a daily, unavoidable act of visibility, a once-and-only idea that no competitor could replicate.

The billboard becomes a sunshine map.

Idea: A billboard that uses real-time data to track every sunny spot in the city, showing people exactly where and when to find sunshine to enjoy a Magnum.
Insight: Even in winter, people enjoy small moments of sunshine — a perfect opportunity to encourage ice cream consumption.

extracted.tagline: Find your summer
extracted.context: Ice cream sales in the UK drop by more than half during the low season, falling from 140M litres to 60M litres.
extracted.insight: Even in winter, people enjoy small moments of sunshine — a perfect opportunity to encourage ice cream consumption.
extracted.idea: A billboard that uses real-time data to track every sunny spot in the city, showing people exactly where and when to find sunshine to enjoy a Magnum.
execution: Digital billboards displaying sunny spot locations and times
Real-time city mapping of sunlight using 3D data
Beacons on billboards sending geo-located push notifications
App notifications directing users to nearest store with Magnum discount
In-context photos of people finding sunny spots and enjoying Magnum

inferred.one_liner: The billboard becomes a sunshine map.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Magnum reframed winter as an ice-cream occasion by turning city sunlight into a real-time media buy, driving significant uplift in off-season sales and earning broad international press coverage.

visual.style_description: Moody monochrome case-study layout built around a hero photograph of a woman standing in a rare patch of street sunlight, surrounded by clean serif headlines, supporting screenshots and 3D city maps. Composition is editorial and cinematic, with high contrast between shadowed architecture and a single illuminated subject.

idea.rationale: Turning a billboard into a real-time sunshine map is a genuinely native outdoor idea — it only makes sense in public space, and it reframes a weather problem as a treasure hunt. The mnemonic is clean: sun + Magnum, here, now. It's not visually iconic in a single glance (it requires the data layer to land), which keeps it just below the very top tier for pure poster memorability.

strategy.rationale: Magnum is Unilever's premium adult ice-cream brand whose mission centres on 'pleasure' as a year-round indulgence, so a campaign that manufactures winter ice-cream occasions is tightly coherent with the brand's hedonistic positioning. The context is hard-data backed and specific: UK ice cream sales drop from 140M to 60M litres in low season, a verifiable category truth. The insight — that people chase small patches of winter sun — is a recognisable cultural behaviour, especially British, that justifies the mechanism rather than being a strategic stretch.

execution.rationale: Media-to-target fit is the strong point: 3D city sunlight mapping feeds digital OOH, beacons trigger geo-located push notifications, and the app routes users to the nearest store with a discount — a closed loop from billboard to purchase. The orchestration runs in three coherent layers (real-time DOOH, mobile push, retail redemption) and is amplified by a broad international press pickup across Times, Guardian, Adweek, AdAge, Fast Company and trade press. Craft is editorial and cinematic in the case film, though the static board itself is more functional than beautiful.

impact.rationale: The headline business result is the right one for this brief — +66.3% sales vs. average winter season — directly addressing the 60M-litre off-season gap rather than a surrogate like impressions. The number is unanchored on absolute volume (66.3% of which baseline, which stores, which window?) so it should be treated as an entrant claim, and the press list is wide but unverified. Still, a sales metric tied to the exact problem stated is materially stronger than the reach-only proofs that dominate this category.

why_it_won.verdict: Gold because the idea is mechanically native to outdoor and closes the loop to a sales lift on the specific seasonal problem it set out to solve — a data-driven DOOH play that actually moved litres, not just impressions.

one_liner: The crushed can IS the ad.
search_doc:
The crushed can IS the ad.
Mechanisms: cultural-symbol-subversion, brand-guideline-break, product-as-medium
Idea: Break strict brand guidelines and crush Coca-Cola's iconic logo on crushed cans to inspire people to recycle every single can, with the 'Recycle Me' call-to-action taken from the side of the can.
Insight: The Coca-Cola logo is recognized by 94% of the world's population.

extracted.tagline: Recycle Me
extracted.context: Coca-Cola has sustainability initiatives including a goal of making 100% of its packaging recyclable by 2025 and collecting/recycling a bottle or can for every one sold by 2030. The brand wanted to spread the recycling message.
extracted.insight: The Coca-Cola logo is recognized by 94% of the world's population.
extracted.idea: Break strict brand guidelines and crush Coca-Cola's iconic logo on crushed cans to inspire people to recycle every single can, with the 'Recycle Me' call-to-action taken from the side of the can.
execution: Hero image of a crushed Coca-Cola can with distorted logo and 'Recycle Me' tagline
Series of print/poster executions each featuring a uniquely crushed Coca-Cola logo
Multilingual versions of the call-to-action: 'Recycle Me', 'Recíclame', 'Recicle-Me'
Cans crushed via different methods: by hand, recycling plant press, and vacuum
press_coverage: AdAge: 'Coca-Cola's logo is indestructible, remaining instantly recognizable even after being crushed.'
Taxi: 'Coca-Cola isn't afraid to mess with its branding for a meaningful cause.'
Fast Company: 'Coke's logo is iconic. The brand just demolished it in a new ad'
Adweek: 'Coca-Cola Crushes Its Logo in New Recycle Me Campaign'
Fernando Machado: 'One of the best things I've seen from Coke in a very long time. Simple and beautiful.'

inferred.one_liner: The crushed can IS the ad.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Earned widespread press coverage by breaking Coca-Cola's sacred brand guidelines to dramatize recycling, repositioning the world's most recognized logo as a call-to-action for sustainable behaviour.

visual.style_description: Bold product photography of a crushed Coke can centered on a saturated red gradient background, flanked by symmetrical columns of press quotes and body copy, with a strip of distorted logo variations along the bottom. Clean editorial layout with high contrast between white type and red field.

idea.rationale: Taking the world's most protected logo and crushing it is a one-glance idea native to outdoor — no headline required to decode it. The mnemonic is the product itself: a deformed Coca-Cola wordmark that still reads as Coke, turning brand recognition into a recycling cue. 'Recycle Me' lifted directly from the can closes the loop with elegant economy.

strategy.rationale: Coca-Cola is a globally dominant beverage brand whose sustainability commitments (100% recyclable packaging by 2025, collect-a-bottle-for-every-one-sold by 2030) make a recycling message coherent with stated mission rather than opportunistic. The insight — that the Coke logo is recognised by 94% of the world's population — is the load-bearing rationale for breaking guidelines: only a logo this universal can survive deformation and still communicate. The 94% figure is a widely-circulated brand claim rather than a fresh study, but it's directionally defensible and the idea-mission fit is tight.

execution.rationale: Craft is disciplined: saturated Coke red, hero product photography, and a typographic system that lets the crushed logo carry the entire message — exactly the right means for a poster format. The series logic (hand-crushed, plant-pressed, vacuum-crushed; multilingual 'Recycle Me / Recíclame / Recicle-Me') extends the device across markets without diluting it, and the PR loop (AdAge, Fast Company, Adweek, Taxi) was clearly engineered into the launch as the 'brand breaks its own guidelines' story. Channel-target fit is strong for OOH because the visual resolves in under a second.

impact.rationale: The board shows no hard business or behaviour-change metrics — no recycling-rate lift, no bottle-return data, no sales or sentiment shift — so impact rests on earned media and industry endorsement (AdAge, Fast Company, Adweek, Fernando Machado's public praise). Those are real cultural-conversation signals for a sustainability message inside the ad industry, but they are surrogate metrics: we don't know if a single extra can was recycled. Reach is plausibly large given Coke's footprint, but unquantified on the board.

why_it_won.verdict: It won Gold because Coca-Cola did the one thing Coca-Cola never does — deform its sacred logo — and that single act of brand sacrifice turned every crushed can in the world into the ad.

inferred.qualitative_summary: Sightwalks expanded the universal language of tactile paving into a destination-finding system, giving visually impaired Peruvians independence in Miraflores and creating an open-source urban standard ready for global adoption.

visual.style_description: Moody black-and-white street photograph shot from a low angle, focusing on a blind person's cane on textured pavement, paired with a clean grid of tactile pattern swatches on the right.

idea.rationale: Extending the universal visual-tactile vocabulary of sidewalk paving into a destination-finding language is a genuinely original mnemonic — counting raised lines to identify a bank, drugstore or bus stop. It's native to outdoor (the sidewalk IS the medium), lands without explanatory text for the user it serves, and has the kind of inevitability great design ideas have: it feels like it should have always existed.

strategy.rationale: SOL is Peru's leading cement brand — a category-niche, national player whose product literally paves cities, so authoring a new tactile paving standard sits perfectly inside its mission and manufacturing competence (cement brand → cement-based accessibility infrastructure). The context is verifiable: standard tactile paving worldwide signals 'walk/stop' but never 'where you are,' a real gap acknowledged by the partnering visually-impaired organizations in Peru. The insight — tactile paving tells the blind where to walk but not where they are — is a cultural-design truth more than a data point, but it's directly validated by the collaborating NGOs.

execution.rationale: Means and media are exquisitely matched: the message lives in the exact surface the target audience already reads with a cane, so channel = product = medium. Orchestration ran from co-design with Peru's largest visually-impaired organizations, to physical rollout across Miraflores district, to user training sessions, to open-sourcing the system for global replication — a proper launch→adoption→amplification arc. Craft is high: the codified tactile vocabulary covers 10 venue types and the documentation board pairs moody street photography with a clean swatch grid that reads instantly to sighted juries and press.

impact.rationale: Tangible, verifiable infrastructure outcomes: +75,000 m² of paved area implemented and +500,000 people benefited in Miraflores — these are absolute figures, not unbased percentages, and the area metric is physically auditable. Press pickup across design (designboom, Design Indaba, TAXI) and Peruvian national media (El Comercio, La República, RPP) extends cultural footprint, and the open-source release creates a replication vector beyond the campaign window. The obvious gap is that SOL's commercial uplift isn't disclosed, but for a purpose play the behaviour-change and infrastructure proof is the right business outcome.

why_it_won.verdict: It wins because SOL turned its own raw material — cement — into a new accessibility language that is simultaneously a product, a public service, and an open-source urban standard; a juror's first sentence is 'the sidewalk became the map.'

one_liner: An unknown granny becomes Spain's biggest subway star.

An unknown granny becomes Spain's biggest subway star.
Mechanisms: ordinary-person-as-celebrity, unsold-inventory-as-canvas, self-promotional-proof-of-medium, platform-as-content
Idea: Hire Marina Prieto, a completely unknown 100-year-old Galician granny with only 28 Instagram followers, and place her posts across unbooked subway ad spaces to see if she could go viral — proving that if she could, any brand could.
Insight: If unbooked subway ad space can turn a complete unknown into a national celebrity, it can do the same for any brand.

More than 850 subway spaces showed Marina gardening, eating churros, or napping
No brand explanation — just waited for public reaction
Visual strip shows multiple subway placements of Marina's Instagram posts across stations

El Español — 'All of Spain is asking: who is Marina Prieto?'
Antena 3 — 'An advertising campaign that changed everything.'

inferred.insight: If unbooked subway ad space can turn a complete unknown into a national celebrity, it can do the same for any brand.
inferred.one_liner: An unknown granny becomes Spain's biggest subway star.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Turned an anonymous Galician grandmother into a nationally recognised cultural figure, generating press, social chatter and cross-border conversation that proved out-of-home's pulling power and convinced advertisers to reinvest in subway media.

visual.style_description: Photographic case board styled like a newspaper front page, with a wide shot of a real subway platform where Marina sits beside her own oversized Instagram-post billboard, surrounded by mocked-up social reactions and press quotes. Dense editorial layout with elegant serif headline typography and a filmstrip of additional placements along the bottom.

idea.rationale: Using a 100-year-old unknown granny as the protagonist of a self-promotional stunt is a charming, counter-intuitive proof-of-medium device — the medium IS the message, executed through a human being rather than a stat sheet. It's memorable in a single glance because Marina's face becomes the campaign, and it's natively outdoor: the joke only works because the subway space itself does the lifting.

strategy.rationale: JCDecaux is the world's largest out-of-home media owner, and its core mission is to convince advertisers that physical OOH still delivers cultural impact in a digital age — making a self-demonstration campaign perfectly coherent with brand mission. The context is data-backed and specific (Spanish subway ad investment down 7% in 2023, unsold inventory available), and the insight — that if unbooked space can make a nobody famous, it can do the same for any brand — is the most honest possible argument a media owner can make. The only soft spot is that the '7% decline' is the sole sourced figure anchoring the problem.

execution.rationale: The means are exactly fit to the objective: 850+ unbooked subway spaces across stations displayed Marina's actual 54 Instagram posts with zero brand explanation, letting curiosity drive the press loop (Cadena SER, El País, Antena 3 all asking 'who is this lady?'). The orchestration is clean — silent launch on dead inventory → organic public bewilderment → press pickup → reveal — and the choice to use literal unsold subway panels rather than premium sites IS the proof point. Craft on the boards (editorial layout, dignified photography of Marina on the platform beside her own poster) is high-end.

impact.rationale: The headline percentages (+39,285% IG growth, +13,405% engagement) are unanchored — 39,285% of 28 followers is roughly 11,000, impressive in context but the absolute base matters and is downplayed. However, the business outcome that actually matters for JCDecaux IS reported and on-brief: 185 new brands onboarded, 2x media investment, and 'record bookings' — these are the real KPIs for a media owner, not surrogate reach metrics. Press in 14 countries extends cultural footprint beyond Spain.

why_it_won.verdict: Gold because JCDecaux turned its own unsold inventory into the most persuasive sales pitch a media owner can make — and backed it with the only metric that counts for them: 185 new advertisers and doubled investment.

inferred.insight: A draw is just numbers on a stage — but a port city can stage it at the scale of the city itself.
inferred.one_liner: The UEFA draw, restaged in shipping containers.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Transformed an otherwise invisible UEFA ritual into a tangible Hamburg landmark moment, putting the port city at the centre of global football coverage and earning international media pickup around the Euro draw.

visual.style_description: Dark nocturnal case-board with a collage of on-site photography arranged around a hero shot of illuminated stacked containers displaying team flags. Dense documentary-style layout with bold black-and-white logo lockup anchoring the top.

idea.rationale: Restaging the UEFA draw as a stack of illuminated shipping containers in Hamburg's port turns an invisible TV ritual into a city-scale spectacle — a mnemonic that lands in one glance: containers = flags = draw. It's native to outdoor (the port IS the medium), category-disruptive against the usual studio-bound draw, and exploits a once-a-year cultural moment of guaranteed global attention.

strategy.rationale: The 'brand' is Hamburg Marketing, the city's destination/place-branding body whose mission is to project Hamburg's identity globally — and the port is the city's most iconic asset, so staging a football moment in containers is a near-perfect mission fit. The context (UEFA draws are watched by millions but viewers can't tell where they're hosted) is a verifiable cultural truth, though the board doesn't cite hard viewership data to size the opportunity. The insight — that a host city should physically claim the broadcast — is sharp and brand-coherent rather than data-led.

execution.rationale: Craft is high: night-time port photography, flag-lit container stacks, and a choreographed real-time build synchronised to the official draw create a TV-ready visual. Orchestration layers the live spectacle with an elevated press platform, on-site guests, and influencer social stories — engineered to be picked up by the global media already covering the UEFA event, which is the right channel for a place-branding play. The parallel-event mechanic makes Hamburg unavoidable in any draw coverage without needing paid media.

impact.rationale: The board claims global media pickup and positions Hamburg at the centre of the Euro draw conversation, which is the right business outcome for a city-marketing brief — but no hard numbers (impressions, earned media value, visitor intent lift, sentiment) are disclosed in the extracted metrics, so the scale is asserted rather than proven. For a place brand the cultural footprint argument is plausible and the visual was demonstrably broadcast-worthy, but the absence of quantified reach caps the score.

why_it_won.verdict: It won Gold because it solved a universal broadcast problem — 'you can't see the host city' — with a single, unmistakable image (a port restaging the draw in its own containers) that made Hamburg itself the logo of the UEFA moment.

extracted.insight: The Irish Heritage Council offers protected status, tax support and financial benefits to places of historical significance open to the public, such as museums.
extracted.idea: Heineken turned historic Irish pubs into museums via AR, making them eligible for tax reductions and protections so their rich past could safeguard their future.

Interactive 3D artifacts inside pubs with descriptions and audio guides
Virtual museum experience guiding visitors through pub history (e.g., Luan's Legend)
Souvenirs and beers available for purchase in-app
Rolled out across historic pubs including Mother Mac's (1787), Duke of York (1772), Sean's Bar (AD 900), Toner's Pub (1818)

LADbible: Explore the history of iconic Irish institutions with Heineken

inferred.one_liner: Historic Irish pubs become protected museums.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Reclassified beloved Irish pubs as legitimate museums, unlocking heritage protections, tax relief, and tourism status that shield them from closure. Turned Heineken into an active guardian of Irish cultural identity rather than just a beverage sponsor.

visual.style_description: Dark, moody pub interior photography centered on a hand holding a phone with the AR museum app, surrounded by dense press logos and supporting case visuals. Layout is information-rich with green accent typography against warm brown tones.

idea.rationale: Reframing endangered Irish pubs as 'museums' to qualify them for Heritage Council protection is a sharp legal-loophole-as-creative-act that turns a category cliché (beer brand sponsors pub) into a structural rescue. The mnemonic — a pint glass as exhibit — lands instantly and is native to the venues themselves, with AR adding a contemporary museum layer without diluting the core thought.

strategy.rationale: Heineken is a global lager brand whose Irish business is umbilically tied to the on-trade pub channel; protecting historic pubs is direct self-interest dressed as cultural stewardship, and the mission-fit is tight because pubs ARE Heineken's distribution and brand theatre in Ireland. The context is verifiable and data-anchored (a quarter of Irish pubs closed since 2005, 157 in the past year), and the insight — that the Irish Heritage Council grants tax relief and protection to publicly-accessible places of historical significance — is a real, exploitable legal mechanism, not a marketing conceit.

execution.rationale: The means fit the objective: AR turns the pub itself into the media channel, with QR-activated 3D artifacts, audio guides and named pub-specific narratives (Sean's Bar AD 900, Mother Mac's 1787, Toner's 1818) giving each venue genuine museum scaffolding rather than a generic overlay. Orchestration ran from on-premise rollout across landmark pubs to a heavy global PR loop (CNN, FT, Telegraph, Bloomberg, Creative Review) — appropriate for a story whose news value is the legal reclassification itself.

impact.rationale: The headline outcome is structural and concrete rather than a vanity metric: participating pubs secured 20% tax relief on building maintenance, heritage protection from buyout, and tourism-guide inclusion — actual policy outcomes, not impressions. The board lacks hard business numbers (Heineken sales lift, footfall to participating pubs, app downloads), so the commercial return is inferred via the press wall, but the cultural and preservation impact is unusually tangible for an Outdoor entry.

why_it_won.verdict: It won Gold because it weaponised a real piece of heritage law to physically save its own distribution channel — a creative idea with a legal-system-level outcome, not just a campaign-level one.

one_liner: The pill in your hand becomes the helpline number.
search_doc:
The pill in your hand becomes the helpline number.
Mechanisms: everyday-object-transformation, purpose-driven-product, hidden-code-hijack, contextual-ooh
Idea: A helpline that converts the mandatory imprint codes on opioid pills into phone extensions (e.g. codeine IP33 dialled as 4733), connecting callers to the recorded story of someone who quit that exact pill plus live expert advice.
Insight: Every opioid pill carries a mandatory imprint code — a unique identifier already in the addict's hand at the moment of greatest temptation.

extracted.tagline: The helpline that turns the object of addiction into a way out.
extracted.context: Every 6 minutes, someone in the US dies from opioids, yet opioids remain widely accessible for medical reasons. If people can't be prevented from getting pills, how can they be prevented from abusing them?
extracted.idea: A helpline that converts the mandatory imprint codes on opioid pills into phone extensions (e.g. codeine IP33 dialled as 4733), connecting callers to the recorded story of someone who quit that exact pill plus live expert advice.
execution: Contextual OOH billboards featuring real pill imprint codes
Website (855-how-to-quit.org) with audio stories like 'How Jason Quit'
Mobile/web experience with recorded recovery testimonials
Social media activism
PSA on national TV
Film content showing callers and former addicts who answer
Bench and street-level contextual placements
metrics: 142 people offered to share recovery stories
6.1M+ Americans suffer from opioid use disorder (context stat)

inferred.insight: Every opioid pill carries a mandatory imprint code — a unique identifier already in the addict's hand at the moment of greatest temptation.
inferred.one_liner: The pill in your hand becomes the helpline number.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Turned the very object of addiction into a direct line to recovery, sparking thousands of calls and a wave of survivors willing to share their stories within weeks of launch. The campaign reframed pill imprint codes as a public health asset and earned broad editorial coverage across major US news outlets.

visual.style_description: Documentary-style photography combined with bold sans-serif typography and real pill props embedded into headlines; layout is editorial and grid-based with contextual OOH imagery anchoring the case.

idea.rationale: Turning the mandatory imprint code on every opioid pill into a phone extension is a genuine code-hijack idea: the object of addiction literally dials the way out. It's a one-glance mnemonic that lands without explanatory copy — the pill, the number, the helpline — and is native to outdoor because the contextual board lets the pill prop and code do the talking.

strategy.rationale: WHO IS THE BRAND? Anzen Health is a niche US health player not internationally known, positioning itself around accessible recovery support — making an anti-addiction helpline mission-coherent rather than opportunistic. The context is real and documented (the 'every 6 minutes' opioid death cadence and 6.1M Americans with opioid use disorder are well-established CDC-range figures), and the insight that every pill carries a unique FDA-mandated imprint code is a verifiable regulatory truth weaponised cleverly. The weakness is that Anzen's authority to own this conversation is asserted more than proven.

execution.rationale: Means fit the objective: contextual OOH near pharmacies and benches puts the code-as-number where temptation happens, while the phone system, recorded survivor stories ('How Jason Quit'), live experts and the 855-how-to-quit.org site complete the call-to-action loop. Orchestration ran from OOH launch into national PSA, social activism and a heavy PR push that earned Bloomberg, AP, Reuters, Forbes, CBS and Fox pickup — a coherent launch→amplify→sustain arc. Craft is editorial and restrained (teal/burgundy palette, real pill props embedded in headlines), appropriate to a healthcare message.

impact.rationale: 137M contacts in 4 weeks is a press-impressions claim and should be read as entrant-reported reach, not verified audience; the harder behavioural numbers — 8,000+ calls and 142 survivors volunteering their stories — are modest in absolute terms against 6.1M sufferers but are the right KPI for a helpline (actual help-seeking behaviour, not awareness surrogates). What's missing is downstream outcome: no data on treatment enrolment, recovery starts, or sustained call volume beyond the launch window, so the cultural footprint outpaces the proven behaviour change.

why_it_won.verdict: Silver because the imprint-code-as-phone-extension is a near-perfect outdoor mnemonic with real behavioural pickup, but the absence of downstream recovery outcomes — and reliance on a 137M impressions headline — kept it short of Gold.

inferred.one_liner: The aid station IS a supermarket aisle.
inferred.qualitative_summary: FreshChoice turned a token sponsorship into a genuine act of service, delighting exhausted runners mid-race and reframing the brand as one that actually helps customers cross the finish line. The stunt earned emotional runner testimonials describing it as a mirage and a hallucination, elevating a regional supermarket into a memorable, purpose-led sponsor.

visual.style_description: A wide hero photograph of a branded blue supermarket aisle planted incongruously on a mossy forest trail, framed by tall native trees, with supporting copy in green sans-serif and a strip of documentary photos along the bottom. Composition is symmetrical and cinematic, balancing lush natural greens with the synthetic blue of the installation.

idea.rationale: Transforming a remote forest aid station into a literal supermarket aisle is a sharp, one-glance idea that fuses the brand's category (groceries) with the runners' precise mid-race needs. The visual gag — synthetic blue retail furniture incongruously planted in mossy native bush — is mnemonic and lands without explanation, and the price-tag copy ('70% off chafing', 'Vaseline $0.00') extends the device with wit.

strategy.rationale: FreshChoice is a New Zealand regional supermarket chain whose positioning centres on local, neighbourhood service rather than scale — not internationally known, but locally read as the 'helpful' grocer. The idea is tightly coherent with that mission: it converts a generic naming-sponsor role into a literal act of stocking and serving customers when they need it most. The insight (runners hit a wall at 50km and need practical supplies, not branding) is a verifiable endurance-sport truth, though no hard data is cited on the board.

execution.rationale: Media-target fit is excellent: the installation sits exactly where the audience is most receptive — physically depleted, 50km in — and the supermarket-aisle build, signage system, price tags and staff handovers are crafted to a high standard, with cinematic hero photography of blue retail against native forest. Orchestration is single-moment rather than phased, but the runner-testimonial layer ('I thought I was hallucinating') gives it a built-in PR loop, and the documentary photo strip suggests content capture for social rebroadcast.

impact.rationale: The board offers no hard numbers — no participant count, no earned-media figures, no brand-tracking shift, no sales or footfall data for FreshChoice stores. Evidence is limited to three runner quotes which, while emotionally vivid, are anecdotal and self-selected; for a regional sponsor the obvious business outcome (basket lift, sponsorship ROI, brand consideration in catchment) is conspicuously absent. Impact therefore rests on plausible reputational gain and charm rather than demonstrated results.

why_it_won.verdict: Silver landed because the idea is a near-perfect one-line transformation of a token sponsorship into genuine brand utility — but the absence of any quantified results capped it short of Gold.

one_liner: The wide receiver IS the 7-Eleven.

inferred.one_liner: The wide receiver IS the 7-Eleven.
inferred.qualitative_summary: 7-Eleven turned an NFL star's offhand self-comparison into a 48-hour merch and gameday moment that dominated American sports media and became one of the brand's best-selling product drops ever. It transformed a fan meme into a co-branded cultural property without a traditional endorsement deal.

visual.style_description: Cinematic nighttime portrait of Ja'Marr Chase arms outstretched, overlaid with a glowing neon-orange 'Always Open' script and flanked by tight columns of press headlines and bold stat callouts. Dense case-study layout balanced by a strong central hero image.

idea.rationale: Hijacking Ja'Marr Chase's self-given '7-Eleven' nickname and turning it into an 'Always Open' co-branded property is a sharp, mnemonic match between athlete behaviour and brand truth (24/7 hours). It's memorable in a glance — the orange/green wordmark on Chase's chest does the heavy lifting — and is native to the gameday-as-billboard environment rather than a film cut-down.

strategy.rationale: WHO: 7-Eleven is the globally recognised convenience chain whose entire brand mission is built around being open around the clock — 'Always Open' is literally the operating promise. The idea is therefore unusually coherent with mission: Chase publicly equated his on-field availability to the brand's core proposition, giving 7-Eleven a free, verifiable cultural insight (his interview quote 'I'm 7-Eleven' is a real, sourced moment, not a manufactured one). The context — an unclaimed nickname gifted by an NFL star — is genuinely data-backed via broadcast and social footage.

execution.rationale: The orchestration is the story: a sub-48-hour reactive turnaround moving from athlete quote → e-comm drop (IsJaMarrOpen.com) → gameday shirt-on-field placement → store rebrands → press loop across ESPN, Sports Illustrated, Washington Post and sports-media long tail. Channels are correctly chosen for an NFL-fan target (live broadcast, sports press, social, merch), and the layers compound: the on-field shirt becomes the media asset that fuels the press cycle that fuels the merch sellout.

impact.rationale: Business outcomes are unusually concrete for a reactive play: collection sold out twice in under 48 hours, 2nd best-selling merch item in 7-Eleven history, and 2nd-highest-ever site traffic — these are real commercial proofs, not surrogates. The '500M impressions' and '18M broadcast viewers' are entrant-claimed reach figures that should be treated as soft, and '99% positive sentiment' lacks a disclosed sample base, but the merch and traffic records carry the case on their own.

why_it_won.verdict: Silver because 7-Eleven turned a free cultural gift into a genuine commercial event in 48 hours with mission-perfect coherence — what holds it back from Gold is that the idea was handed to the brand by the athlete rather than constructed by the agency, and the headline reach numbers lean on unverified impression claims.

inferred.qualitative_summary: Coors Light turned an accidental Ohtani foul ball into a global cultural moment, scoring an unofficial baseball sponsorship and entering the Japanese market for the first time — all without paying for rights or endorsements.

visual.style_description: Photo-driven case board with a hero shot of the special edition can held against a stadium backdrop, flanked by an action photo of Ohtani batting. Clean sans-serif typography with blue callout banners and a strip of supporting execution thumbnails along the bottom.

idea.rationale: Turning a literal flaw — a black square punched into the billboard by Ohtani's foul ball — into the new can design and an unofficial MLB sponsorship is a textbook reactive-marketing idea that reads in a glance. The mnemonic is built-in: the black square is the brand's new shorthand for 'Ohtani touched this,' and it works across can, billboard and stadium screen without explanation.

strategy.rationale: WHO IS THE BRAND: Coors Light is a Molson Coors mainstream American lager whose brand mission centres on refreshment, casual sport-watching culture and easy-going Americana — a beer category where MLB rights belong to a competitor (Budweiser is the Official Beer of MLB). The idea is highly coherent with that mission: leaning into an actual baseball moment lets Coors Light play in baseball culture without paying for category sponsorship. The insight ('a broken ad is proof a superstar interacted with the brand') is grounded in a verifiable, publicly documented incident — Ohtani's foul ball hitting the billboard was real news — so the context is solid even if no formal data study is cited.

execution.rationale: Phasing was tight and channel-native: within 48 hours a replica 'damaged' can was in market, then the black square was retro-fitted onto existing OOH and Angel Stadium screens, then social/PR carried the meme globally, then the cans were exported to Japan to ride Ohtani's home-market fame. Craft on the hero board is clean (can hero + Ohtani action shot + execution strip), and the channel choice — OOH at the site of the incident, amplified by earned social — matches a sport-watching audience exactly where they already are.

impact.rationale: Claimed outcomes are directionally strong: sold out in under 24 hours (though base volume isn't disclosed), trended higher than the Official Beer of MLB (platform and window unspecified), '100% positive sentiment' (sample unstated), and a first-ever entry into the Japanese market — the only hard business outcome on the board. Press pickup (AdAge, LA Times, Campaign) supports cultural footprint, but the absence of absolute sales lift or share-shift numbers caps how far Impact can go despite the obvious virality.

why_it_won.verdict: Silver because the black-square-as-sponsorship turnaround is a genuinely original reactive idea executed in 48 hours, but the impact case leans on sentiment, sell-out and trend claims without the hard sales or share numbers that would have pushed it to Gold.

inferred.insight: Fans don't want more sponsors on their jersey — they want fewer. A brand that respects the kit earns more love than one that plasters its logo on it.
inferred.one_liner: The sponsorship IS the absence of sponsorship.
inferred.qualitative_summary: By buying jersey ad space only to leave it blank, Consul flipped the logic of sports sponsorship and won fans' affection across Brazilian football culture, earning massive press coverage and a measurable lift in brand love and sales.

visual.style_description: Case-board layout with a clean cream background, gold serif display type, and two large hero jersey renders shown as before/after, flanked by editorial body copy and a grid of in-match photography. Composition is symmetrical and editorial, evoking a premium sports magazine spread.

idea.rationale: Sponsoring a jersey by erasing every logo — including its own — is a sharp, single-glance inversion of the entire sports-sponsorship category, perfectly native to outdoor because the jersey IS the medium. The before/after comparison lands instantly without explanation, and 'the sponsorship is the absence of sponsorship' has genuine mnemonic stickiness in a category drowning in logo clutter.

strategy.rationale: Consul is a Brazilian home-appliance brand (Whirlpool-owned, washing machines) whose product promise centres on cleanliness — so 'cleaning' a jersey is a literal, on-brand metaphor rather than a borrowed-interest stunt. The context — Brazilian clubs stacking up to 25 sponsors on iconic kits, with fans visibly resenting it — is a verifiable cultural truth in Brazilian football, though the board leans on cultural shorthand rather than citing a specific fan study or data source to anchor the insight.

execution.rationale: The means fit the objective precisely: buying every ad slot on Juventus SP's kit and leaving it blank across 20 official 2024 matches turns each game broadcast and stadium appearance into an earned-media trigger, with in-match photography and the before/after hero shot doing the explanatory work. Craft is high — editorial layout, premium typography, clean hero renders — and the PR loop (Reuters, ESPN, L'Equipe, Marca, Clarín) was clearly engineered as the amplification layer, though the board shows little phased orchestration beyond launch + press pickup.

impact.rationale: The +12% sales lift is the meaningful business outcome for a washing-machine brand and it is directly named, which is the strongest signal here; +2300% brand mentions and +400% brand love are unbased percentages (of what prior volume?) and 28M earned media is an entrant claim, though the breadth of the press list (Reuters, CNN, ESPN, L'Equipe, Marca, Olé) gives it credibility. Cultural footprint is real within football media but the campaign's behaviour-change claim on the sponsorship category itself is asserted, not demonstrated.

why_it_won.verdict: Silver landed because a washing-machine brand found the one sponsorship gesture that was both literally on-brand (clean) and a category-wide provocation (anti-logo), backed by a real sales number — but the absence of a verifiable insight source and unbased love/mention percentages kept it out of Gold.

one_liner: Irish pubs become protected museums.
search_doc:
Irish pubs become protected museums.
Mechanisms: cultural-symbol-subversion, legal-loophole-exploit, ar-overlay, heritage-preservation
Idea: Heineken turned historic Irish pubs into museums via augmented reality, making them eligible for tax reductions and protections so their rich past can protect their future.
Insight: The Irish Heritage Council offers protected status, tax support and other financial benefits to places of historical significance open to the public, such as museums.

extracted.tagline: Turning historical pubs into real museums
extracted.context: Irish pubs face a challenging economic environment: since 2005, a quarter of Irish pubs have shut. 157 closed in the past year, while no Irish museum has had the same problem.
extracted.insight: The Irish Heritage Council offers protected status, tax support and other financial benefits to places of historical significance open to the public, such as museums.
extracted.idea: Heineken turned historic Irish pubs into museums via augmented reality, making them eligible for tax reductions and protections so their rich past can protect their future.
execution: AR app where users scan a QR to activate augmented reality in the pub
Interactive 3D artifacts with descriptions and audio guides (e.g. 'Luan's Legend')
Pub map to visit participating Pub Museums
Ability to buy beers and souvenirs in-app
Rollout across historic pubs: Mother Mac's (1787), Duke of York (1772), Sean's Bar (AD 900), Toner's Pub (1818)
metrics: Economic evaluation of cultural heritage collection
press_coverage: Creative Review: How Heineken is using AR to help preserve Irish pubs
LADbible: Explore the history of iconic Irish institutions with Heineken
CNN: Heineken Celebrates Historic Irish Pubs With Virtual Museums Launch
Financial Times: Heineken aims to safeguard historical Irish pubs

inferred.one_liner: Irish pubs become protected museums.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Heineken reclassified vanishing Irish pubs as museums, unlocking 20% tax cuts, legal protection from buyouts, and inclusion in national tourism guides — using AR to safeguard cultural heritage through legislation rather than marketing.

visual.style_description: Dark, moody pub interior photography anchored by a hero shot of a hand holding a phone displaying the AR app, with green accent typography and a structured info-graphic layout. Dense case-study board with clear hierarchy between idea, execution, and results.

idea.rationale: Reframing pubs as museums to exploit heritage legislation is a sharp legal-loophole-exploit idea that turns a cultural problem (157 pubs closed last year) into a structural solution. The mnemonic is instantly graspable in a single line — 'pubs become museums' — though the AR layer is more enabler than headline image, making it slightly less native-to-outdoor than a pure poster idea.

strategy.rationale: Heineken is a globally known lager brand whose Irish-market identity is deeply tied to pub culture — protecting pubs is direct protection of its on-trade distribution and brand habitat, so the idea is highly coherent with its commercial mission. The context is data-backed (a quarter of Irish pubs closed since 2005; 157 in the past year) and the insight is a verifiable mechanism: the Irish Heritage Council genuinely grants tax relief and protections to publicly-accessible heritage sites. The strategic chain from problem → legal lever → brand role is unusually tight.

execution.rationale: The means fit the objective: AR via QR turns each pub into a self-contained museum experience (3D artefacts, audio guides, 'Luan's Legend'), with a pub-map and in-app commerce sustaining repeat visits across landmark sites (Sean's Bar AD 900, Mother Mac's 1787, Toner's 1818). Orchestration runs launch in heritage pubs → legal reclassification → global PR pickup (CNN, FT, Telegraph, Bloomberg). Craft on the board is high — moody pub photography, green accent typography, clear hierarchy — though the AR interface itself reads as functional rather than beautifully crafted.

impact.rationale: The headline outcomes are structural rather than promotional: a 20% tax cut on maintenance, protection from buyouts, and inclusion in the national tourism guide — concrete, verifiable policy wins for participating pubs. However, the board shows no business outcome for Heineken itself (no sales lift, no footfall data, no share-of-voice number), and the press list is a logo wall without impression figures, so cultural reach is asserted rather than quantified. Impact is real but narrow — a handful of pubs protected — which caps the scale claim.

why_it_won.verdict: Silver landed because the legal-loophole idea is genuinely elegant and brand-coherent, but the absence of quantified reach or business results — and the small number of pubs actually reclassified — kept it off Gold.

campaign: If you're into it, it's in the V&A

Whatever you're into, it's already in the V&A.

Idea: Hero the breadth of the V&A's collection by proving it has something for everyone, targeting one passion at a time — from mainstream to super niche — with authentic creators and world-first stunts.
Insight: People dismiss museums as irrelevant to their personal passions, not realising their niche obsessions already live inside the collection.

inferred.one_liner: Whatever you're into, it's already in the V&A.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Repositioned the V&A from an intimidating institution into a museum that mirrors every Brit's personal obsession, driving a massive surge in collection searches and earning broad cultural and press attention across mainstream and niche media.

visual.style_description: Dense photo-led case board mosaic mixing product close-ups, OOH placements, in-the-wild stunts, and social screenshots, anchored by bold yellow sans-serif headlines on black.

idea.rationale: 'If you're into it, it's in the V&A' is a sharp, sticky proposition that turns the museum's intimidating breadth into a personal mirror for every niche. The mnemonic — putting the actual collection object into the obsessive's native habitat (Warhammer cafés, driving ranges, GTA Online) — lands in a glance without explanation, and is genuinely native to outdoor and ambient rather than a film flattened to poster.

strategy.rationale: The V&A is the UK's national museum of art, design and performance, whose mission is to make its 2.8M-object collection relevant and accessible to all — an idea that proves the collection contains 'you' is tightly coherent with that mission. The context is data-backed (only 19.3% of UK adults visited a museum in the last year, citing irrelevance) and the insight — that people don't realise their obsessions already live in the collection — converts that stat into a creative wedge. The percentages are sourced to a real cultural-participation issue rather than invented.

execution.rationale: Means and media are unusually well-matched to target: each niche audience is hit in its own habitat (philatelist stamps, metal-detectorist buried silverware with QR, gamer GTA jacket hack, knitter store sweaters), with credible creator collaborators (@Hookednbusy, @MeOldChina, London Embroidery Studio) doing the craft so executions feel native, not advertised. Orchestration runs from hyper-targeted ambient stunts → creator social rebroadcast → mainstream and niche press pickup (Angling Times alongside TIME), and the visual system — yellow-on-black wordmark plus the actual collection object — holds hundreds of executions together coherently.

impact.rationale: The +12,000% rise in 'collection searches' and +23% in V&A searches are the most on-strategy proofs (people actually interrogating the collection), though '12,000% of what base?' is undisclosed and likely off a tiny baseline. 37M impressions and the press roster (TIME, CNN, BBC, Rolling Stone) are entrant-claimed reach rather than verified, and the obvious business outcome — visitor footfall to the museum — is conspicuously absent from the board. Cultural footprint across mainstream and niche press is real, but the business case is surrogate-heavy.

why_it_won.verdict: Silver landed because the idea-to-execution fit is exceptionally precise — each niche stunt is a perfect proof of the line — but the absence of a hard footfall or membership number, and percentage gains without disclosed bases, kept it from Gold.

inferred.one_liner: Shot on iPhone, but drawn with pencils.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Hijacked Apple's most iconic ad format to reposition Faber-Castell pencils as instruments capable of photographic realism, asserting handcraft as a defiant counterpoint to AI-generated advertising.

visual.style_description: Hyperrealistic colored-pencil drawings staged inside Apple-style minimalist OOH layouts, dominated by a giant close-up eye centerpiece with clean white framing and sans-serif typography. Case board mixes polished product mockups, process shots, and environmental placements with generous whitespace.

idea.rationale: Hijacking Apple's most recognisable ad format — 'Shot on iPhone' — to argue that a pencil can rival a camera is a sharp, single-glance idea that needs zero explanation in OOH. It lands instantly because the layout itself is the mnemonic; the twist of 'Shot on Faber-Castell' carries cultural weight by also positioning handcraft as a quiet rebuke to AI-generated imagery.

strategy.rationale: Faber-Castell is a 260-year-old German manufacturer of fine art pencils whose mission is championing craftsmanship and artistic expression — the idea is tightly coherent with that mission, since the campaign literally demonstrates the artisanal capability of its Supersoft range. The cultural context (Apple's decade-old campaign, the rise of AI imagery in advertising) is real and verifiable, and the insight that premium pencils in expert hands produce photoreal results is proven on the poster itself. The strategic gap is that the 'pushback against AI' framing is asserted rather than evidenced with any category data.

execution.rationale: Craft is the load-bearing element and it delivers: hyperrealistic colored-pencil renderings of an eye, oranges, zebra, cat and bread executed by 5 artists using 100+ colors over 400+ hours, dropped into pixel-accurate Apple-style minimalist layouts. Placement in subway corridors, backlit transit panels and bus stops mirrors exactly where 'Shot on iPhone' lives, reinforcing the parody at point of contact; process films of the artists drawing provide a natural social amplification layer. Multi-execution coherence is strong across the series.

impact.rationale: The board offers production metrics (5 artists, 100+ colors, 400+ hours) but no reach, earned media, sales, or brand-lift figures — these are inputs, not outcomes. For a parody of one of the world's most famous campaigns, the absence of any PR pickup numbers or business result is conspicuous, so impact rests on inferred cultural resonance from the craft rather than demonstrated results.

why_it_won.verdict: Silver because the Apple-format hijack is instantly legible and the pencil craft genuinely earns the photographic comparison — but the absence of any verifiable reach or business outcome kept it from climbing into Gold territory.

inferred.insight: Existing tactile paving tells the blind where to walk, but never where they are.
inferred.one_liner: The sidewalk becomes the signage.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Turned a cement brand into an accessibility infrastructure pioneer, giving Peru's visually impaired community true navigational independence and creating an open-source urban standard with global replication potential.

visual.style_description: Documentary-style black-and-white photograph of a visually impaired person's legs and cane on tactile paving, overlaid with clean white typography and grid of grey tactile-code icons on the right. Dense informational poster with strong real-world authenticity.

idea.rationale: Extending tactile paving into a navigational vocabulary is a genuinely original act of urban semiotics — it turns the sidewalk itself into wayfinding signage, native to outdoor and instantly memorable. The mnemonic is the infrastructure: counting lines with a cane to identify a bank or pharmacy is a single, tangible mechanic that lands without explanation for the user, even if the poster itself needs a legend.

strategy.rationale: SOL is Peru's leading cement brand — not internationally known — and its mission credibly extends to shaping the built environment, so a tactile-paving innovation is squarely coherent with what it actually makes and sells. The context (existing tactile paving guides walking but never destination) is a verifiable cultural and design truth, reinforced by partnership with Peru's biggest organisations for the visually impaired, which anchors the insight in lived user input rather than agency assertion. The board would be stronger with a stat on Peru's visually impaired population, but the underlying problem is self-evident.

execution.rationale: The means fit the objective: a cement brand intervening in cement is the right medium, deployed in Miraflores with user-training sessions so the vocabulary is actually learnable, not just symbolic. Open-sourcing the system is a deliberate amplification layer designed to travel beyond Lima, and the press orchestration spans design press (designboom, Design Indaba), national news (El Comercio, La República, TV Perú) and trade — a coherent multi-tier rollout. The poster craft itself is clean documentary B&W with an icon grid, functional rather than dazzling.

impact.rationale: 75,000 m² implemented and 500,000 people benefited are concrete infrastructure numbers, not vanity impressions — the 500K figure is a catchment claim rather than a verified user count, but the square-metre figure is auditable and unusually tangible for an Outdoor entry. What's missing is any SOL business outcome (brand lift, sales, share) and longitudinal evidence of replication in other cities, which caps the cultural-footprint claim of an 'open-source global standard' at potential rather than proven.

why_it_won.verdict: Silver because the idea redefines a piece of public infrastructure as brand-coherent accessibility signage with real square-metres on the ground — but it stops short of Gold because the global open-source ambition and SOL business impact remain promissory rather than demonstrated.
