inferred.qualitative_summary: Alianza Lima exposed online grooming by twisting football's child-escort tradition, sparking a 500% surge in reports and prompting parents across Peru to confront the issue with their children.

visual.style_description: Documentary-style match photography with players lined up on the pitch, one holding hands with a visibly adult man wearing a kid-sized kit. Dark stadium backdrop with bold white headline typography and structured left-side info blocks.

idea.rationale: Hijacking football's child-escort ritual to reveal an adult holding the captain's hand is a sharp, single-glance visual metaphor for online grooming — adults posing as kids. The mnemonic is strong and category-native to live sport, though the device leans on understanding the FIFA/UNICEF protocol, which is more legible to football audiences than to general outdoor viewers.

strategy.rationale: Alianza Lima is Peru's most popular football club, not an international brand, and child protection is not a core part of its mission — the club is borrowing moral authority rather than extending an existing platform. The insight is anchored in concrete national data (280,000 minors propositioned online, 500,000 meeting strangers offline), which gives the context verifiable weight, but the brand-mission coherence is opportunistic rather than intrinsic.

execution.rationale: Staging the stunt during a live, televised match — the exact moment audiences expect the child-escort ritual — is the right channel for the message, and the post-match family photo opportunities extended the gag into a second beat. Craft is solid documentary sports photography, and sponsor/media collaboration provided an amplification layer, though the board doesn't show a sustained multi-phase rollout beyond the matchday moment and press loop.

impact.rationale: The headline '+500% increase in reports' is unbased — 500% of what baseline, on which reporting platform, over what window? The $270M earned media value and 8M impressions are entrant-claimed PR surrogates rather than verified behaviour change, and while the press quotes suggest genuine national conversation, the load-bearing behavioural metric lacks the absolute numbers a jury needs to credit it as society-level impact.

why_it_won.verdict: A clever ritual-hijack with a real insight that earned its shortlist on idea clarity, but the unbased '+500% reports' figure and the borrowed-cause brand fit kept it below the Bronze line.

visual.style_description: Hyper-minimal photographic close-ups of the Golden Arch with a single glossy milkshake drip running down, shot with condensation droplets to evoke summer heat. Clean, confident OOH composition with no copy, letting the icon and drip carry the whole message.

idea.rationale: A confident wordless poster that turns the Golden Arch into the lip of a cup and a single coloured drip into the milkshake flavour — pure distinctive-asset play. Memorable in a glance and native to outdoor, but it is essentially a logo + drip — a well-executed brand-asset flex rather than a new creative leap, and McDonald's has mined this wordless-icon territory many times before (Logistics, Follow the Arches, etc.).

strategy.rationale: McDonald's is globally known; its mission here is everyday craveability and reactive cultural presence, and a heat-triggered milkshake teaser is coherent with that. The insight — that the Arch alone is iconic enough to carry meaning — is a defensible cultural truth backed by decades of brand-asset research, but the board offers no specific data (no recognition stats, no heatwave traffic data) and the 'surprise UK heatwave' context, while real, is a thin trigger rather than a deep insight.

execution.rationale: Craft is the strongest pillar: glossy photographic drips, condensation beads, three flavour variants (strawberry, banana, chocolate) sharing one disciplined visual system that reads instantly from a bus stop or 48-sheet. Placement in UK OOH during the heatwave is well-timed and target-relevant, and the wordless system clearly travelled into design/ad press — but there is no evidence of orchestrated phases, partnerships, or amplification beyond the posters and resulting editorial pickup.

impact.rationale: The board lists an impressive press roster (Creative Review, The Drum, Campaign, Adweek, AdAge, Mashable, LAD Bible, Mirror, Mail Online) but provides zero hard metrics — no milkshake sales lift, no footfall, no brand-tracker movement, not even impressions. For a QPR-style reactive campaign the obvious business outcome is milkshake sales during the heatwave window, and its conspicuous absence means impact rests entirely on trade-press admiration.

why_it_won.verdict: A textbook distinctive-asset poster that jurors clearly respected for craft and confidence, but with no business results and a device McDonald's has used many times before, it had nowhere to go beyond shortlist.

Little Black Book: 'WhatsApp created a first-of-its-kind stunt by installing OOH shades to protect the privacy of the current tenants'
TAXI: 'The out-of-home campaign relieves tenants of these homes from prying eyes'

inferred.one_liner: The billboard IS the window shade.
inferred.qualitative_summary: The stunt turned a brand message into a genuine act of service for residents of famous TV locations, earning widespread press coverage and driving WhatsApp's privacy sentiment in the US to an all-time high while boosting downloads and active users.

visual.style_description: Bold case-study board with a vivid green left panel featuring chunky black sans-serif typography, chat-bubble framing devices, and registration marks, paired with a documentary photo grid of real OOH placements annotated with WhatsApp message bubbles.

idea.rationale: Turning the billboard INTO the window shade is a clean, native-to-OOH metaphor: the shade literally performs privacy while advertising it. The 'Full House', Tom's Restaurant and 'New Girl' window choices are culturally legible at a glance, though the device is a single visual joke repeated three times rather than escalating, and 'privacy shades' as a stunt has precedent in the ambient-object-as-message tradition.

strategy.rationale: WhatsApp is a Meta-owned global messaging app whose stated mission centres on end-to-end encrypted, private communication — a positioning it has been pushing hard in the US where it lags iMessage. The idea is tightly coherent with that mission: the brand earns the right to talk about privacy by literally providing it to real tenants. The insight — that famous TV-location residents are harassed by tourists — is a real, widely reported cultural truth (Full House house owners have publicly complained for years), though the board doesn't cite hard data.

execution.rationale: Media choice is the message: physical shades on three iconic, pilgrimage-worthy facades in SF, NYC and LA guarantee tourist photography and organic social rebroadcast, which is the real amplification channel. Craft on the board is high — green chat-bubble system, consistent typographic lockup, documentary photography of tourists in-frame — and the three-city rollout gives geographic coherence. What's missing is a clear phased orchestration beyond the install-and-photograph loop (no partnership, influencer or PR layer is shown).

impact.rationale: The board claims 23M new private messages, 917k new MAUs, 192k downloads and an 'all-time high' in US privacy sentiment in Q3 — directionally on-strategy metrics, but 917k MAU and 192k downloads are modest for a Meta-scale product and none are baselined (% of what, vs. what prior quarter?). 'All-time high in privacy sentiment' is an unsourced sentiment claim, and there's no attribution model tying these numbers to three physical shades rather than concurrent paid media. Reach is essentially the PR pickup (Ad Age, LBB, TAXI) shown on the board.

why_it_won.verdict: A neat, brand-mission-perfect OOH metaphor with high craft, but the stunt is a one-note visual repeated three times and the results, while on-message, are too modest and unbaselined to push it past Shortlist into Bronze.

The stated insight (popcorn:cinema :: Lay's:sports) is a credible cultural analogy rather than a data-sourced truth, and the board doesn't cite specific consumption or viewership studies to anchor it — so the strategy is coherent but lightly evidenced.

execution.rationale: Craft is high: 171 crew, 8 cameras, a drone, jumbotron integration, and two perfectly-cast legends staging a chip argument pitch-side — the orchestration of live stadium moment → broadcast capture → social/PR rebroadcast is textbook. impact.rationale: Claimed +150M views and +2.5B PR impressions are load-bearing entrant figures with no third-party verification, and '+100% positive sentiment' is mathematically opaque (100% of what baseline?). why_it_won.verdict: A polished celebrity-led stadium stunt that earned its shortlist on craft and scale, but didn't medal because the core idea is a sponsorship activation dressed as Outdoor rather than a glanceable, mnemonic Outdoor invention.

one_liner: The football pitch becomes feminine in Arabic.
search_doc:
The football pitch becomes feminine in Arabic.
Mechanisms: cultural-symbol-subversion, language-as-medium, environment-as-canvas

extracted.tagline: The World's First Football Pitch to Have a Female Gender
extracted.context: Young girls in the Middle East grow up believing sports aren't for women, and most give up sports by age 14. Ironically, in Arabic — which predates these cultural beliefs — most sports-related words have a female gender.
extracted.insight: In Arabic, the female gender of a word is created by adding a letter at the end: ة (Ta' Marbuta) — a circle with two dots on top.
extracted.idea: In partnership with Onyx FC (UAE's first all-female owned and operated football team), two dots were added above the center circle of their pitch, turning it into a Ta' Marbuta and creating the world's first football pitch with a female gender.
execution: Aerial view of the football pitch transformed with two dots above the center circle to form the Arabic Ta' Marbuta
Onyx FC purple kit and club crest featuring female players
Players on the modified pitch during training/matches
Photo strip showing other community sports fields and courts later modified with the same two-dot symbol across Dubai

inferred.one_liner: The football pitch becomes feminine in Arabic.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Reframed a male-dominated sporting space through Arabic grammar itself, inspiring communities across Dubai to replicate the two-dot mark on their own fields and signalling that girls have always belonged in sport.

visual.style_description: Hero aerial 'tiny planet' photograph of the pitch anchors a dense editorial case-study layout, flanked by supporting photography, Arabic typography specimens, and clean sans-serif body copy on a deep navy background.

idea.rationale: Adding two dots above the centre circle to convert the pitch into the Arabic Ta' Marbuta is a smart linguistic-visual pun that lands in a single aerial glance — it is native to outdoor and mnemonic. However, the device only fully reads to Arabic literates who recognise the grammatical mark, and it requires the aerial vantage point to function, which limits its in-the-field legibility for the average passer-by.

strategy.rationale: Onyx FC is the UAE's first all-female owned and operated football club, so its mission is explicitly to legitimise women's participation in football in the Gulf — the idea of grammatically feminising the pitch is tightly coherent with that mission. The insight is a verifiable linguistic fact (Arabic sports vocabulary carries feminine gender via Ta' Marbuta) layered onto a real cultural problem (girls dropping sport by 14), though the board cites the drop-off as assertion rather than sourced study.

execution.rationale: Craft on the hero aerial is high and the two-dot mark is reproducible, which enabled the key amplification phase: 38+ community fields and courts across Dubai were subsequently re-marked with the symbol, turning a single pitch into a distributed OOH network. The orchestration — branded pitch as launch asset, club matches as live activation, community replication as earned amplification — fits the target, though the board shows limited evidence of paid media or partnership scaffolding beyond the club itself.

impact.rationale: The 165% sign-up increase is the most relevant business metric for a football club recruiting female players, but the absolute base is undisclosed — 165% of what cohort? The 220M reach across 19 countries is an unverified entrant claim, and the 38 replicated fields is the most tangible cultural-footprint proof, though modest in absolute terms.

why_it_won.verdict: A genuinely elegant linguistic insight executed with restraint, but the idea requires an aerial view and Arabic literacy to fully land, and the impact numbers lean on unbased percentages rather than a hard business outcome — enough to clear the shortlist screen, not enough to medal.

The jealous-toddler-glaring-at-newborn shot is relatable but not category-breaking — Burger King has trained the industry to expect sharper visual provocations.

execution.rationale: The means are coherent with the objective: documentary-style real-family photography on OOH, bus stops, in-store crown activation and press created a sibling-day moment families could see and act on. Craft is competent and the orange/beige BK system is consistent across executions, but orchestration is fairly standard (cultural-date launch + in-store promo + press) with no notable PR loop, partnership, or amplification layer beyond owned channels.

impact.rationale: The +36% incidence on Kids Meals and +2pp uplift vs previous promo are real business outcomes directly tied to the campaign's objective of family engagement, which is the right metric to cite. However, 227M impressions is an unverified entrant claim, the +36% base is undisclosed (36% of what baseline volume?), and there is no evidence of cultural conversation beyond a Daily Mail line and two tweets — solid but not Lion-level proof.

why_it_won.verdict: A warm, on-brand insight executed competently with genuine sales lift — but the idea never crystallises into a single glance-readable outdoor mnemonic, which is exactly what separates an Outdoor shortlist from an Outdoor Lion.

The insight that kids feel heroic in games while parents are absent IRL is culturally resonant but the board cites no data (hours gamed, parental-absence stats) to anchor it, leaving the premise as intuition rather than evidence.

execution.rationale: Craft is the strongest layer: the black-and-white documentary photography, the chromatic-aberration ghosting on the 'opponent', the cursor triangles and the hoop-net framing translate the gaming HUD into a poster idiom with real polish. As a standalone print/OOH execution it is coherent and on-target for parents in public space, but there is no visible orchestration, partnership, or amplification layer beyond the single board.

impact.rationale: No reach, sales, engagement, or behaviour-change figures are presented — and for a telco the obvious business outcome (subscriber metrics, brand consideration lift) is conspicuously absent. inferred.qualitative_summary: Eletromidia converted Brazil's largest out-of-home advertising network into a real-time emergency search system, pausing paid media the instant a child goes missing nearby. The initiative mobilized up to a million people in active searches and reframed commercial public screens as civic infrastructure for child safety.

visual.style_description: Cinematic, photo-driven layout blending a haunting close-up of a child's face with documentary-style shots of digital OOH screens in public spaces, anchored by oversized bold sans-serif typography and a targeting-reticle logo. Dense editorial composition with clear case-study sections, map visualizations, and a strip of execution stills.

idea.rationale: Repurposing a paid OOH network into a real-time AMBER-alert system is a clean, one-glance idea native to outdoor — every screen becomes a missing-child poster. However, the mechanic (digital screen hijack for a social cause) has been seen before in adjacent categories, so it lands as smart and useful rather than genuinely novel, which caps its medal potential.

strategy.rationale: Eletromidia is Brazil's largest OOH media operator — not a consumer brand but an infrastructure player whose mission is the relevance of public screens; donating that inventory to a child-safety cause is highly coherent with proving the civic value of its network. The context is anchored in a verifiable FBSP statistic (135+ children missing daily in Brazil) and the insight about critical first-minutes is a documented truth in missing-persons protocols, giving the strategy solid data backing.

execution.rationale: The means fit the objective tightly: a real-time data pipe from local authorities triggers ~5,000 screens in malls, corridors, escalators and street totems, with a search radius expanding every 10 minutes — exactly where crowds and children intersect. The board shows multi-format coherence (app → mall screens → totems), but the orchestration is essentially a single mechanism activated on trigger; there's little evidence of PR loops, partnership layers or sustained amplification beyond the screens themselves.

impact.rationale: The headline numbers — 11.5M impressions, $1M in donated media, up to 1M people 'mobilized' — are reach/surrogate metrics, not outcomes: the board does not disclose how many missing children were actually found via the system, which is the only business/social outcome that matters here. '1M mobilized' is also an unanchored claim (mobilized = walked past a screen?), so impact reads as scale of inventory donated rather than lives demonstrably changed.

why_it_won.verdict: A strategically coherent, well-crafted media-donation idea that screened in for its civic logic and scale of activation, but stalled at shortlist because the board reports inventory donated and eyeballs reached instead of the one number that would have unlocked a Lion: children actually recovered.

one_liner: The family adopting the most kids in Mexico is the cartel.

The family adopting the most kids in Mexico is the cartel.

Print ads with QR codes calling to 'Help us place them in a safe environment'

inferred.one_liner: The family adopting the most kids in Mexico is the cartel.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Reframed cartel recruitment as a brutal form of adoption, exposing how everyday family rituals mask criminal grooming and rallying public support for safer environments for Mexican youth.

visual.style_description: Cinematic, warmly-lit documentary-style portraits with shallow depth of field and muted earth tones, paired with elegant serif typography on a deep black layout. Composition mimics tender family snapshots, subverted by the menacing adult figures surrounding each boy.

idea.rationale: Reframing cartel recruitment as 'adoption' is a sharp, emotionally loaded conceptual flip that lands in a glance via the subverted family-snapshot composition. However, the device — captioned 'He is not...' portraits — is a relatively familiar NGO trope (the subverted tender moment), and the visual still leans on the line to fully unlock, which softens its native-outdoor punch.

strategy.rationale: Save the Children is a globally recognised child-welfare NGO whose mission is to protect children from violence and exploitation — placing cartel grooming squarely within its remit, so idea-mission fit is tight. The insight that criminal groups substitute for absent family via everyday rituals (driving lessons, football, TV) is a credible cultural truth in Mexico, and the board anchors it to a cited 100,000–200,000 figure of recruited youth, though the source of that range is not specified on the board itself.

execution.rationale: Craft is genuinely high: cinematic warm-lit portraits, restrained serif typography and a disciplined black layout give the series a coherent gallery-grade finish across three executions. Media choice (bus shelters, transit) reaches the working-class commuters whose children are most at risk, and the QR-to-help mechanism closes the loop — but there is no visible amplification layer, partnership, or phased rollout beyond the print/OOH itself.

impact.rationale: The board provides no metrics, no press coverage, no donation or sign-up figures, and no evidence of behaviour or policy shift — the obvious business outcomes for an NGO (donations, volunteer sign-ups, programme referrals via the QR) are conspicuously absent. Without any reach, scan-rate or cultural-conversation proof, impact rests entirely on the assumed resonance of the creative, which a jury cannot reward.

why_it_won.verdict: A craft-strong, strategically tight poster series whose conceptual flip earned it past the screening — but with zero results disclosed and a device that doesn't fully escape NGO-poster convention, the jury had nothing to push it from Shortlist into Bronze.

one_liner: The happy animal logo becomes a confession.

inferred.one_liner: The happy animal logo becomes a confession.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Turned competitors' own mascots into anti-meat advertising by exposing the irony of cheerful animal logos, driving plant-based trial and doubling NotCo sales within two weeks.

visual.style_description: Case-study board layout with a large central hero photo of a hand holding a phone scanning a 'Pollo Feliz' storefront, surrounded by supporting thumbnails and clean sans-serif copy blocks. Photo-driven, moderately dense, with a cinematic dusk tone.

idea.rationale: Turning competitors' own happy-animal logos into confessions of animal suffering is a sharp piece of symbol-subversion, and the 'Pollo Feliz' scan lands the irony in a glance. However, the idea requires a phone scan plus a song to fully deliver — it's not pure outdoor, and the logo-hijack mechanic has precedents in activist work, limiting its uniqueness.

strategy.rationale: NotCo is a Chilean food-tech challenger brand whose mission is replacing animal products with plant-based equivalents via AI-formulated recipes — the anti-meat provocation is directly coherent with that mission. The insight ('happy animals sell dead animals') is a real, observable cultural contradiction visible on thousands of storefronts across MX and US, anchored in the verifiable fact that 40 logos and 15,000 stores carry these mascots. The strategic logic — attack category iconography from the outside rather than build NotCo's own — fits a challenger's economics.

execution.rationale: The means (webapp scan triggering a song + discount code redeemable on NotCo products) is well-matched to the target: shoppers physically standing at the meat counter are intercepted at the decision moment, which is the right channel for conversion. Craft is competent — cinematic dusk photography of the Pollo Feliz scan, clean typography — but the board shows a single mechanic across 40 logos with no clear phased orchestration, PR loop, or amplification layer beyond the in-store scan itself.

impact.rationale: The headline '+116% sales in two weeks' and '+76% scans-to-purchase' are concrete business outcomes tied to the brand's real objective (plant-based trial), which is stronger than impression-based surrogates. However, both figures lack a baseline — 116% of what prior period, in which markets, against what SKU set? — and there is no disclosed press pickup or cultural-conversation evidence to extend the impact beyond the activation window across the claimed 15,000 stores.

why_it_won.verdict: A genuinely smart logo-hijack insight executed as a competent scan-to-discount mechanic — shortlist-worthy provocation, but it medals only if the idea lands purely in outdoor space, and here it depends on a phone, a song and an unbaselined sales claim.

inferred.one_liner: The upside-down shelf IS the better price.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Plaza Vea turned a tiny, universal shopper gesture into a citywide visual language for low prices, making its 'every day low prices' promise instantly recognizable and ownable in a category full of generic price claims.

visual.style_description: Documentary-style street photography of large red billboards mimicking supermarket shelves with products dramatically inverted, paired with clean monospaced white typography on dark asphalt. Realistic, high-fidelity product photography with strong retail color cues.

idea.rationale: Flipping products upside-down on billboards to mimic the universal shopper gesture of checking a price tag is a smart, ownable visual device that reads in a glance without copy. However, 'upside-down product = low price' is a well-trodden retail trope (the literal 'price drop'), so the freshness comes mostly from the behavioral framing rather than from a truly novel mnemonic.

strategy.rationale: Plaza Vea is Peru's largest supermarket chain (Intercorp Retail), whose positioning leans on 'precios bajos todos los días' — an everyday-low-price promise that the idea translates literally into a visual code, so brand-mission coherence is tight. The insight — shoppers physically flip competitor products to compare labels/prices — is a plausible cultural-truth observation but is not backed on the board by any sourced study or shopper data, leaving it as an asserted behavior rather than a verified one.

execution.rationale: Craft is solid: red Plaza Vea brand blocks, clean monospaced typography and high-fidelity product photography of inverted Tide, wine, fish, snacks and dishwashing bottles make each board read instantly from the street. The campaign is essentially a single-channel OOH run across multiple city placements with category variations for coherence, but there is no evidence of phased orchestration, in-store tie-in, or amplification layer beyond the billboards themselves.

impact.rationale: The board provides zero metrics — no reach, no footfall lift, no sales movement, no share-of-voice, no social pickup. For a retailer whose obvious business objective is store visits and basket share, the absence of any business outcome is conspicuous and caps Impact at a 'presumed local visibility' level.

why_it_won.verdict: A neat, on-brief visual pun that earned its shortlist on craft and brand-fit, but the device is too close to the well-known 'price drop = upside-down' trope and the board carries no results — not enough lift to clear the Bronze line.

inferred.qualitative_summary: Positioned CNN as the uncompromising global watchdog by tying its breaking-news identity directly to the actions of the world's most scrutinised leaders, reinforcing brand authority amid an era of press distrust.

It lands in a glance, yet it doesn't leave a sharp visual hook beyond the chyron-as-frame, which many news brands have used before.

strategy.rationale: CNN is a globally recognised news brand whose mission is round-the-clock international reporting, so positioning itself as the watchdog of Trump, Maduro and Putin is coherent with that mandate. However, the board offers no sourced insight or data point — no trust study, no audience figure, no specific context about press freedom — so the strategic underpinning rests on a generic 'journalism matters' assertion rather than a verifiable truth.

execution.rationale: Craft is high: the photojournalistic compositions, restrained gray editorial layout and the chyron typography mimic broadcast stills convincingly, and the triptych is internally coherent across the three leaders. As outdoor, though, the frames are dense and reportage-heavy — they read more like magazine spreads than glanceable posters, and there's no evidence of placement logic, phasing, or amplification beyond the three prints.

impact.rationale: No metrics, no press pickup, no awareness or sentiment shift are presented on the board. For a media brand whose business objective would be viewership or trust lift, the absence of any audience or perception data leaves impact entirely unevidenced.

why_it_won.verdict: A well-crafted but conventional news-brand flex: the chyron-over-leader device is competent enough to clear the screening bar, but lacks the originality or proof of impact a juror needs to push it to Bronze.

one_liner: The Mayo Chicken burger, minus everything but the mayo.

The Mayo Chicken burger, minus everything but the mayo.

Idea: Although McDonald's doesn't serve mayo as a dip, it serves it inside the Mayo Chicken burger. The Mayo McHack invited people to customise the burger by de-selecting chicken, lettuce and bun — leaving just the mayo — and Hellmann's reimbursed the £1.19 cost for anyone posting with #MayoMcHack.

extracted.context: Mayonnaise is the UK's number one condiment, especially loved with fries. But McDonald's, the nation's biggest fries seller, doesn't offer mayo as a dip, claiming insufficient demand. As the no.1 mayo brand, Hellmann's decided to act.
extracted.insight: Mayonnaise is the UK's number one condiment and people especially like it with their fries, yet McDonald's doesn't offer it as a dip.

The Mayo McHack invited people to customise the burger by de-selecting chicken, lettuce and bun — leaving just the mayo — and Hellmann's reimbursed the £1.19 cost for anyone posting with #MayoMcHack.
execution: Print ads featuring the Mayo McHack box
OOH billboards outside McDonald's locations ('The New Mayo Chicken')
Social posts on Hellmann's channels ('Psst… we found the secret sauce')
User-generated content: TikToks and photos of fans recreating the hack with fries and mayo
metrics: One cease & desist letter from McDonald's
press_coverage: Contagious: 'A category-leader flex that asserts Brits' love of mayo'
McDonald's (Rik Vandewall, Director Global Marketing): 'Well played!'

inferred.one_liner: The Mayo Chicken burger, minus everything but the mayo.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Hellmann's exploited a loophole in McDonald's own menu to deliver mayo dip to UK fries fans, sparking viral participation and forcing McDonald's to respond with a cease and desist — proof the demand they denied was very real.

visual.style_description: Clean product-led case board with a hero shot of a McDonald's-style box containing a swirl of mayo beside golden fries, framed by social media screenshots, press quotes, and a strip of UGC thumbnails. Bold navy slab typography on a warm beige background gives it a confident, retail-poster feel.

idea.rationale: Hijacking McDonald's own Mayo Chicken burger to extract just the mayo as a fries dip is a witty, category-leader prank with a clear one-line mnemonic. It's memorable and culturally cheeky, but the visual device (a McDonald's-style box of mayo) needs the backstory to fully land — it doesn't quite read 'in a glance' from a billboard without copy support.

strategy.rationale: Hellmann's is Unilever's global mayo leader whose brand mission centres on championing mayo's role in everyday eating and reducing food waste; weaponising a competitor's menu to defend mayo's #1 condiment status is coherent with that 'mayo evangelist' posture. The insight — that mayo is the UK's top condiment yet McDonald's, the country's biggest fries seller, refuses to offer it — is a verifiable cultural truth, though the board leans on the claim without citing the specific consumption study behind it.

execution.rationale: The orchestration is tidy: OOH outside McDonald's locations seeded the hack, print and social ('Psst… the secret sauce') instructed the ritual, and a £1.19 reimbursement mechanic converted passers-by into UGC creators on TikTok. Channel-target fit is sound for a young, social-first fries audience, but the OOH layer feels more like a PR prop than a standalone outdoor idea — this is really a social/PR stunt with billboards attached.

impact.rationale: +320% social engagements and +46% brand consideration are directional but unanchored — 320% of what baseline, over what window? The cease-and-desist from McDonald's is the strongest proof point, signalling genuine cultural traction, but there's no sales or volume figure for Hellmann's jars, which would be the real business outcome; the +9pt purchase intent is a soft surrogate.

why_it_won.verdict: A clever competitor-hijack with a quotable cease-and-desist payoff earned the shortlist, but as an Outdoor entry it reads as a PR stunt with billboards bolted on rather than an idea that lives natively on the street — which is why it didn't medal.

The idea is coherent with that mission (claim authority on Break Dance by 'proving' it was always there), but the insight is a clever creative conceit rather than a data-backed truth: no studies, cultural stats, or audience data are cited to anchor why Colombian sports fans needed this reframing.

execution.rationale: Craft is high: clean isolation of athletes against saturated monochrome floors, disciplined typography, and three coherent variants (vault, handball, volleyball) that hold up as a series. Media choice — bus stops, metro digital screens, magazines, mobile — fits a mass sports-fan target in the run-up to the Games, but there is no evidence of orchestrated phasing, PR loop, or amplification layer beyond standard paid placements.

impact.rationale: The board reports zero metrics — no reach, no viewership lift for Win Sports' Olympic coverage, no social pickup, no sentiment or consideration shift. For a broadcaster whose obvious business KPI is tune-in during Paris 2024, the absence of any audience or share-of-voice figure leaves impact entirely unproven and resting on the craft of the posters themselves.

why_it_won.verdict: A charming, well-crafted visual pun that earned its shortlist on poster-craft alone — but a one-joke idea with no data, no amplification, and no results was never going to clear the Bronze bar.

visual.style_description: Split-screen photographic composition contrasting neon-lit nightlife with bright daytime elderly activities, layered with bold neon-green typography and a hot-pink 'Open All Ages' badge. Dense editorial layout with documentary-style imagery, grid of venue examples, and signage close-ups.

idea.rationale: Time-sharing nightlife venues with seniors — nightclubs as morning chess clubs, dance floors as opera halls — is a memorable reframing that lands in a glance via the night/day split-screen. The 'Open All Ages' sign is a smart mnemonic device subverting standard Open/Closed signage. However, the concept needs the night-vs-morning juxtaposition to fully read, making it slightly more editorial than poster-native.

strategy.rationale: The 'brand' here is Shanghai Municipality, a city government whose mission includes social inclusion and managing an aging population — the idea is tightly coherent with that civic mission. The context is grounded in verifiable data points cited on the board: 5.4M elderly (1/4 of Shanghai's population) and 5,000+ entertainment venues, with land scarcity preventing new senior facilities. The insight — that young and old occupy the same city on opposite clocks — is a genuine cultural truth, not a manufactured one.

execution.rationale: Execution spans five named venues (The BOXX, UNDERGROUND, PK Bar, Club Party King, Club Hood), each with a documented night/morning pairing, plus a custom signage system that gives the initiative a unified visual identifier across locations. The dense editorial layout with neon-green type and hot-pink badges has craft polish, but the board leans on grid-of-examples documentation rather than a single iconic outdoor execution, and the phased orchestration (merchant recruitment, brand coalition, municipal adoption) is implied rather than visualized. Channel fit is strong — the message lives literally on the venue doors the seniors will walk through.

impact.rationale: Claimed results — 250K+ elders benefited, 1.7K+ merchants, 52 supporting brands, 37.6M media impressions — are substantial and the behaviour-change outcome (seniors actually entering these spaces) is the right business metric for a civic inclusion initiative, supported by named testimonials. However, '250K+ benefited' is undefined (visits? unique users? registrations?) and the impression figures are unverifiable entrant claims. The strongest impact signal — that this became an official long-term municipal program — is asserted but not evidenced on the board.

why_it_won.verdict: A genuinely warm, culturally-resonant civic idea with a clean mnemonic sign system — but the board documents a program rather than delivering a single arresting outdoor execution, which is why it cleared the screening without converting to metal.

inferred.insight: The most iconic sign of construction sites, 'MEN AT WORK,' silently excludes the growing number of women in the industry and normalizes sexism in the workplace.
inferred.one_liner: 'Men at Work' becomes 'People at Work.'
inferred.qualitative_summary: Rewrote one of construction's most iconic signs into a symbol of inclusion, spreading across highways, worksites and cities, and ultimately prompting Salvador's mayor to decree the new signage mandatory as part of a wider gender equity program.

visual.style_description: A cinematic, high-contrast portrait of a female construction worker in a yellow hard hat holding the new diamond-shaped 'People at Work' sign, with a crossed-out old sign floating beside her. Dense case-study layout with yellow caption boxes, supporting photos and sponsor logos arranged around the hero image.

idea.rationale: Swapping 'MEN AT WORK' for 'PEOPLE AT WORK' is a clean, legible language-rewrite that lands in a glance — exactly the kind of mnemonic device Outdoor rewards. However, inclusive-language sign-swaps are now a familiar gender-equity playbook (Vienna's Ampelfrau, 'fireperson', etc.), so the originality ceiling is capped even if the execution is locally fresh.

strategy.rationale: The brand is Instituto Mulher em Construção, a Brazilian NGO whose mission is to support and grow the presence of women in civil construction — the idea is perfectly coherent with that mission since it attacks the most ubiquitous symbol of the industry's male default. The insight (1 in 10 Brazilian construction workers is now a woman, yet signage still says 'Homens Trabalhando') is a verifiable cultural truth, though the board doesn't cite the source study, leaving the stat as a soft claim.

execution.rationale: Media choice is on the nose: the message lives on the exact object it critiques, deployed across worksites, highways, subways and bike lanes — right channel, right context. Orchestration extends from physical rollout to a policy loop (Salvador mayoral decree mandating the new signs), which is a strong amplification layer, though the case board's dense yellow-box layout leans more case-study than poster-craft.

impact.rationale: 14,000 signs replaced and a municipal decree are tangible, structural wins that go beyond surrogate metrics, and 'daily impact on 12M people / 2.5M vehicles' are plausible exposure proxies though unverified. The +53% in Institute supporters lacks a disclosed base (53% of what number?), and there is no evidence of attitudinal shift among construction workers or women entering the trade — the obvious behaviour-change outcome for an NGO of this mission.

why_it_won.verdict: A clean, mission-perfect symbol swap that earned its shortlist on policy traction and scale, but the idea sits inside a well-worn inclusive-language genre and the results stop at signage counts rather than proving women's lives in construction actually changed.

one_liner: The security guard IS the fastest sprinter in France.
search_doc:
The security guard IS the fastest sprinter in France.
Mechanisms: celebrity-as-prop, retail-stunt, cultural-symbol-subversion, earned-media-stunt
Idea: As a running store first, Distance brought sport back into sportswear by inviting customers to literally steal items from the store — on the condition they could outrun the security guard, who was secretly Mickael Zeze, the fastest French sprinter.
Insight: Hype culture has hijacked sportswear — resellers and hypebeasts hoard the gear, while the actual athletes it was made for can't get their hands on it.

inferred.insight: Hype culture has hijacked sportswear — resellers and hypebeasts hoard the gear, while the actual athletes it was made for can't get their hands on it.
inferred.one_liner: The security guard IS the fastest sprinter in France.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Reclaimed sportswear for actual athletes by turning a retail drop into a footrace, generating massive global press coverage and reframing Distance as a runner-first brand in a market dominated by hype culture.

visual.style_description: Cinematic motion-blurred action photography of a runner mid-stride dominates the layout, paired with film-still thumbnails and a hand-tagged price label as the central graphic device. Dense editorial case-study composition with bold yellow headlines on a dark street backdrop.

idea.rationale: Turning a retail drop into a literal footrace against a disguised national sprinter is a sharp, memorable mechanic that dramatises 'sport back into sportswear' in one stunt. However, as an Outdoor entry it's fundamentally a retail/PR stunt rendered as a case-study board rather than an idea that lands in a glance from public space — the twist requires explanation to read.

strategy.rationale: Distance is a French specialist running retailer (not internationally known) whose mission is to serve actual runners, so staging a stunt that rewards only those who can physically outrun a sprinter is tightly coherent with the brand's runner-first identity. The insight — that hypebeasts and resellers hoard exclusive sportswear drops, leaving athletes shut out — is a real and well-documented cultural tension in sneaker culture, though the board cites no hard data (resale stats, stockout figures) to anchor it beyond the qualitative claim that only 2 of 100 attempts succeeded.

execution.rationale: The means fit the objective: an in-store stunt with 'Rob It To Get It' tags, a celebrity-sprinter-as-guard reveal, and a wrap-up film engineered for press pickup — exactly the right mechanic for a small running retailer with no media budget. Orchestration is a clean launch → film release → global PR loop, and craft on the case film (motion-blurred action photography, bold yellow tagging) is high; the weakness is that this is an experiential/PR play dressed as Outdoor, with limited true OOH placement logic.

impact.rationale: Claimed 40M views, 750M impressions and €12M earned media 'for the price of two pairs of shoes' is a strong budget-to-reach ratio if the figures hold, and the press list (Colbert, Washington Post, Sky News, Adweek) is genuinely tier-1. However, the board offers no business outcome for Distance — no sales lift, no footfall, no new-customer data, no brand consideration shift — so impact rests entirely on surrogate reach metrics, which is exactly why this sat below the medal line.

why_it_won.verdict: A clever, press-friendly retail stunt that won global earned media but stayed at Shortlist because it's a PR idea competing in Outdoor, with impressions in place of any verifiable business outcome for the brand.

The Times Square countdown becomes the Kia EV9 commercial.

inferred.one_liner: The Times Square countdown becomes the Kia EV9 commercial.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Kia turned the world's most-watched countdown into a live product demo, reframing the EV9's Onboard Power Generator as the hero of New Year's Eve and driving record pre-launch demand and organic social conversation.

visual.style_description: Dramatic nighttime photography of the towering Times Square countdown tower dominating the center, flanked by a clean dark-mode case-study layout with numbered screenshots and embedded social posts. Cinematic, high-contrast composition with glowing amber bulbs against deep black.

idea.rationale: Hijacking the Times Square countdown by killing the main billboard and reviving it with the EV9's Onboard Power Generator is a clever product demo dressed as cultural ritual — the mechanic literally dramatises the feature being sold. But it's still a paid-billboard takeover at an event already saturated with brand stunts, and 'car powers screen' is a known auto-show trick rather than a category-redefining mnemonic.

strategy.rationale: Kia is a globally recognised mass-market automaker pivoting its mission toward EV leadership, and the EV9 launch hangs on proving credibility against Tesla/Rivian — so showcasing the Onboard Power Generator (a genuine differentiator) is coherent with the brand's EV push. The inferred insight — that the countdown is the one moment a billion eyes lock on a single screen — is a real cultural truth, and the 1M on-site / 1B viewership figures are independently established for NYE Times Square. However, no proprietary consumer insight is offered beyond 'big audience = big opportunity', which is thin.

execution.rationale: Craft is high: cinematic nighttime photography, the suspense beat of the blackout one minute to midnight, and the EV9 stepping in as 'saviour' is well-choreographed for live broadcast capture across CNN/ABC/CBS/Hulu. The orchestration — live TV moment → social reaction clips → product page traffic — is logical and target-fit for an automotive launch chasing reservations. What's missing is a deeper OOH layering beyond the single hero moment; it's essentially one stunt amplified, not a sustained outdoor system.

impact.rationale: 6,319 pre-orders and 5.2% retail share pre-launch are real, board-visible business outcomes tied to the obvious objective (EV9 reservations), which is stronger than pure reach claims. The '1 billion watching live' is the standard NYE audience estimate, not incremental Kia reach, and 'highest traffic on the EV9 page in one day' is unbased without a baseline. Tangible but modest for a stunt of this cost — no evidence of cultural conversation outlasting January 1.

why_it_won.verdict: A well-crafted live product demo at the world's biggest screen, but the jury read it as a paid media takeover with a neat feature gag rather than an idea that earned its billion eyeballs — enough for shortlist, not enough for metal.

one_liner: A negative tax that cancels the tax on second-hand.
search_doc:
A negative tax that cancels the tax on second-hand.
Mechanisms: purpose-driven-product, policy-hack, cultural-symbol-subversion, naming-as-protest
Idea: IKEA introduced SHT (Second-Hand Tax) at -13% to cancel out the 13% HST, meaning customers pay 0% tax on As-is (second-hand) items.
Insight: Sustainable consumer behaviour is unfairly penalised by a tax system that charges sales tax twice on the same item.

extracted.tagline: SHT - Second-Hand Tax: A counter tax that eliminates the unfair double tax on second-hand items
extracted.context: Canada is a global leader in sustainability, but Canadians are punished for sustainable choices: buying second-hand items incurs HST sales tax even though tax was already paid the first time — paying tax twice on the same item.
extracted.idea: IKEA introduced SHT (Second-Hand Tax) at -13% to cancel out the 13% HST, meaning customers pay 0% tax on As-is (second-hand) items.
execution: In-store posters featuring As-is products (HATTÅSEN table, KRYSSMAST lamp, HEMNES chest) with green SHT stickers
Open letter newspaper ad showing HST 13% / SHT -13% = 0% receipt
In-store circular signage: 'Oh SHT!', 'This SHT is huge.', 'SHT just got real.'
Petition to the Canadian government to change tax law
metrics: 1st consumer brand in Canada to propose changes to tax laws

inferred.insight: Sustainable consumer behaviour is unfairly penalised by a tax system that charges sales tax twice on the same item.
inferred.one_liner: A negative tax that cancels the tax on second-hand.
inferred.qualitative_summary: IKEA Canada turned a cheeky acronym into a legitimate policy challenge, prompting the Canadian government to agree to meet on amending the double-tax on second-hand goods and rallying retail and tax bodies behind legislative change.

visual.style_description: Clean case-study board layout with a bold yellow-and-black SHT logo lockup, product cutouts, newspaper open-letter photography, and green circular in-store signage echoing IKEA's brand system. Composition is grid-based, information-dense but orderly.

idea.rationale: Naming a counter-tax 'SHT' is a cheeky linguistic hack that lands the double-tax injustice in one glance, and the yellow-and-black logo lockup is mnemonic. But the joke needs the receipt math (13% - 13% = 0%) to fully decode, so it's not purely poster-native — it leans on supporting copy to deliver the policy point.

strategy.rationale: IKEA is a globally known furniture retailer whose stated mission centres on affordability and sustainability, and the As-is second-hand programme is a direct extension of that circular-economy commitment — so brand-mission fit is tight. The context (Canadian HST applies again on resold goods despite tax already paid once) is a real, verifiable feature of Canadian tax law, giving the insight a factual spine. The board does not, however, cite consumer data on how many Canadians know or care about this double-tax — the insight is logical rather than data-backed.

execution.rationale: Craft is clean and on-brand: in-store posters on As-is products, green circular signage punning on the acronym ('Oh SHT!', 'This SHT is huge.'), and an open-letter newspaper ad showing the receipt math — all coherent with IKEA's visual system. Orchestration runs store → press → petition → government lobbying, which is a logical PR loop, but the outdoor footprint itself is modest (mostly in-store and a newspaper page) rather than a public-space takeover, which is a limitation for an Outdoor entry specifically.

impact.rationale: The '192% increase in second-hand sales' is unanchored — 192% of what base volume? As-is is a small slice of IKEA Canada's business, so the percentage could mask a small absolute number. 32,000 petition signatures is concrete but modest for a national policy push, and the 'first consumer brand to propose tax changes' and government-meeting claim are real cultural footprint but stop short of actual legislative change. Press pickup is broad across Canadian outlets but remains a claim, not verified reach.

why_it_won.verdict: A smart, on-brand policy-hack with a memorable acronym that earned its shortlist on idea and brand-fit coherence — but the in-store-plus-newspaper footprint is light for an Outdoor Lion and the headline sales figure is an unanchored percentage, which kept it below the medal line.

inferred.one_liner: A zoo campaign drawn entirely by children.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Granby Zoo turned its own young audience into its art directors, producing a charmingly authentic campaign that lifted attendance and earned massive earned media for a regional attraction competing against Montreal's tourist hubs.

visual.style_description: A clean billboard photograph against a deep blue sky, juxtaposing crude child-drawn tiger illustrations in a before/after split with a polished zoo logo centerpiece. A child's hand with colored pencil extends into frame, reinforcing the handmade conceit.

idea.rationale: Handing the crayon to children for a zoo campaign is a charming, on-brand creative conceit, and the before/after device cleverly dramatises learning through repeat visits. But 'kids draw the ad' is a well-worn trope in family-attraction advertising, and the single-poster joke lands instantly without leaving a strong mnemonic beyond the cute scribble.

strategy.rationale: Granby Zoo is a regional Québec zoo roughly 80km from Montreal, whose mission is family-friendly wildlife education and conservation — putting children's drawings on the poster is highly coherent with that educational, family-first remit. The stated context (tourists default to Montreal, kids love drawing + animals) is a plausible cultural truth but is asserted rather than backed by sourced data or studies on the board.

execution.rationale: Craft is deliberately naive on the drawings but polished in the surrounding frame — clean blue sky, centred logo, a child's hand entering frame to authenticate the conceit — which reads cleanly from a roadside glance. However, the case shows essentially one billboard variant with no visible orchestration into amplification layers (social, in-park activation, partnership with schools), which limits the outdoor system to a single beat.

impact.rationale: +20% visitor lift is a genuine business outcome aligned with the brand's footfall objective, though '20% of what base' is undisclosed and the regional starting point may be modest. The 73M impressions figure is a self-reported reach claim large for a regional Québec zoo and should be treated as an entrant claim rather than verified; no sentiment, earned-media or sustained cultural-conversation evidence is shown.

why_it_won.verdict: A charming, mission-coherent idea with a real footfall result, but the execution is a single cute poster rather than a layered outdoor system — enough to clear the shortlist sanity check, not enough to convince the room it deserved a Lion.

one_liner: The billboard you smell, not see.

visual.style_description: Cinematic street photography of a large unbranded red billboard dominating an urban plaza, with sidebar copy and bold yellow metric callouts in a clean editorial case-study layout. A filmstrip of news and reaction stills runs along the bottom.

idea.rationale: Turning a brand's smell into the entire creative — no logo, no copy, just red-and-yellow color blocks emitting fry scent — is a clean, memorable demonstration of brand-asset confidence. However, scented OOH is not a new mechanic (Dunkin', IKEA and others have done scent-based outdoor), so the originality is in the debranded execution, not the underlying device, which caps the freshness.

strategy.rationale: McDonald's is the world's most recognizable QSR brand whose mission centers on familiarity, ubiquity, and feeding the many; using sensory shorthand to prove its brand assets transcend the visual is fully coherent with that identity. The insight — that the fry smell is as iconic as the arches — is presented as cultural truth but the board offers no recognition study or sourced data to back it; it leans on a plausible assertion rather than evidence.

execution.rationale: Craft is tight: bold unbranded red panels placed within 5m of restaurants create a clear smell-to-store funnel, with proximity-triggered scent diffusion as the mechanism and PR pickup as the amplification layer. The phasing (install near restaurants → press discovers the 'mystery boards' → global news loop) is logical and well-matched to a mass QSR audience, though it's essentially a one-execution stunt rather than a multi-layered orchestration.

impact.rationale: Claimed 1.5B reach, 50M earned media, 25M impressions and 87% positive sentiment are entrant-supplied and unverified, and the absolute base for sentiment and engagement is unclear. Critically, the obvious business outcome — incremental footfall or fry sales at the adjacent restaurants, which the mechanic is literally designed to drive — is absent from the board, leaving only surrogate PR metrics as proof.

why_it_won.verdict: A charming, confidently debranded brand-asset flex that earned a shortlist on idea clarity and press traction, but failed to medal because scent-OOH isn't novel enough and the board never proves the one business outcome the stunt was built to drive: people actually walking in for fries.

Hate it.' identity — so a contraband operation is highly coherent with brand mythology. Channel-to-target fit is sharp (expat subreddits, NYC pub postering), but as an Outdoor entry specifically, the poster craft is deliberately lo-fi 'fly-posting' — appropriate to the contraband aesthetic but unremarkable as standalone outdoor craft.

impact.rationale: 1,200+ smuggler applications is a tangible behavioural metric and on-strategy, and a 26% rise in US Marmite searches is a directional cultural-curiosity signal — but '26% of what base?' is undisclosed, and 5.6M earned reach is modest and unverified beyond UK tabloid pickup (Daily Star, Yahoo, regional Live sites). No sales lift, no US distribution outcome, no expat-community size shift — the obvious business outcome (US availability or demand) is absent.

why_it_won.verdict: A charming brand-true stunt that screened through on idea coherence and PR craft, but it shortlists rather than medals because it's a campaign idea forced into an Outdoor frame — the posters are recruitment utilities, not a poster that wins the category on its own merit.

inferred.qualitative_summary: Sidestepped Singapore's ban on anti-hangover claims by encoding the product's function into a single wordless visual, successfully launching a Japanese brand into a new market and establishing a pre-drinking ritual among nightlife consumers.

visual.style_description: Bold flat-vector illustration of a figure tipping a bottle into their mouth, rendered in deep navy on a warm cream background; minimalist poster aesthetic deployed consistently across OOH, in-store and merchandise.

idea.rationale: The wordless visual of a figure tipping a bottle into their mouth is a clean regulatory workaround that encodes the 'drink before you drink' ritual without naming the cure. But the device is a fairly literal pictogram of product usage — memorable enough at point-of-sale but not a category-redefining mnemonic, and the visual language (flat navy figure on cream) feels closer to packaging illustration than to an idea that lands in a single public glance.

strategy.rationale: Solmack is a leading Japanese anti-hangover remedy (Kowa) attempting Singapore entry — a non-international brand whose mission is hangover prevention, which the wordless ritual idea maps onto coherently. The context is verifiable and specific: Singapore's HSA prohibits anti-hangover marketing claims, a genuine regulatory constraint that justifies the workaround. The insight about nightlife pre-drinking ritual is plausible but not data-backed on the board — it's asserted rather than evidenced.

execution.rationale: Channel choice is on-target: pubs, bars, KTV lounges and liquor shops put the message at the exact moment of intent, with bottle toppers and QR codes converting attention at point-of-sale. However, orchestration is essentially a single key-visual rolled across OOH, in-store and merch — coherent but one-note, with no phasing, partnership layer, or amplification beyond the venue footprint. Craft is competent flat-vector poster work, not distinctive enough to elevate the rollout.

impact.rationale: The 533% sales increase is the headline, but '533% of what?' is unanswered — a launch from a near-zero base in a new market makes the multiplier easy to inflate. The 873 pre-orders in one night and 3,757 QR scans in a day are concrete but small-scale venue metrics, and 'sold out' in-store promotion lacks denominator. Reasonable launch traction, but no cultural footprint, behaviour-change evidence, or reach figures that would justify a Lion.

why_it_won.verdict: Shortlisted as a tidy regulatory-workaround launch with a clear ritual idea, but the visual device is too literal and the results too small-scale-and-unbased to clear the Bronze threshold.

Poster featured BÄSTIS lint roller product with price and instructions to 'roll dust off your finery here'
Norwegians in traditional bunads rolling themselves against the billboard
People in suits also using the sticky surface to clean their clothes

inferred.insight: Wool bunads worn on Norway's National Day are magnets for lint and dust, leaving Norwegians fussing over their finery in public.
inferred.one_liner: The billboard IS a giant lint roller.
inferred.qualitative_summary: IKEA inserted itself into Norway's most beloved national ritual by turning outdoor media into a functional product demo, letting citizens physically interact with the BÄSTIS lint roller at scale and reinforcing IKEA's role as a practical helper in everyday Norwegian life.

visual.style_description: Case-study layout with a large hero photograph of the sticky billboard in use, supported by a grid of documentary-style action shots and annotated callouts. Clean editorial typography on a subtle textured lint-paper background reinforces the product story.

idea.rationale: Turning a 15 sqm billboard into a working giant lint roller is a clean one-liner that lands in a glance — product, function and benefit collapse into a single object. It's native to outdoor and mnemonic, but the 'billboard-as-giant-version-of-product' device is a well-trodden OOH trope (IKEA itself and others have done variants), which caps its originality.

strategy.rationale: IKEA is a globally known home-goods brand whose mission centres on practical, affordable solutions to everyday domestic annoyances — and BÄSTIS, a €2 lint roller, fits that mission exactly. The cultural insight is real and verifiable: Norway's 17 May bunad tradition is a documented national ritual, and wool's tendency to attract lint is a genuine, observable problem the campaign solves in public. The link between brand, product and ritual is tight and honest, though the board cites the cultural truth rather than any sourced data.

execution.rationale: Placement is the strongest execution choice: Oslo's busiest railway station on National Day puts the sticky surface exactly where bunad-wearers transit, making channel and target perfectly aligned. Craft is clean — clear product callout, price, and instructional copy turn the site into a self-explanatory demo — but the campaign appears to be a single-site, single-day activation with no visible orchestration of phases, partnerships or amplification layers beyond organic selfies.

impact.rationale: The board provides no metrics — no footfall, no BÄSTIS sales lift, no PR reach, no social numbers — so the obvious business outcome (driving sales of a €2 product and IKEA store affinity) is entirely unproven. Impact rests on the implied charm of people rolling themselves against a wall and a presumed PR halo, which is a thin evidentiary base for an Outdoor Lion.

why_it_won.verdict: A charming, on-brand product-demo-as-media that screened through on idea clarity and placement wit, but stalled below Bronze because it's a familiar OOH device executed once, with zero results disclosed to lift it above competent.

one_liner: Autocrats' first speeches sound like democracy's promises.
search_doc:
Autocrats' first speeches sound like democracy's promises.
Mechanisms: archival-document-as-evidence, juxtaposition-reveal, long-copy-typography, cultural-symbol-subversion
Idea: For its 30th anniversary, RSF Germany exposes the hopeful words used by modern autocrats in their first presidential speeches—Putin, Maduro, Erdoğan—juxtaposed with images of journalists persecuted shortly after.
Insight: There is a gap between the beautiful promises of modern autocrats when first elected and the ugly truth they later inflict upon democracies.

extracted.tagline: The loss of freedom is never obvious at first. Trust the free press. Not pretty words.
extracted.context: 2024 is a pivotal year for democracy with over 80 countries holding elections. While some may gain more freedom, others face the risk of losing it. The free press is what can stand against the enemies of democracy.
extracted.insight: There is a gap between the beautiful promises of modern autocrats when first elected and the ugly truth they later inflict upon democracies.
extracted.idea: For its 30th anniversary, RSF Germany exposes the hopeful words used by modern autocrats in their first presidential speeches—Putin, Maduro, Erdoğan—juxtaposed with images of journalists persecuted shortly after.
execution: Long-copy print posters featuring first speeches of Putin (Russia 2000), Maduro (Venezuela 2013), and Erdoğan (Turkey 2014)
Digital out-of-home displays across Europe
Magazine spreads
Photography of persecuted journalists (e.g., journalist detained in Moscow 2012; journalist arrested in Istanbul 2021)
QR code driving donations

inferred.one_liner: Autocrats' first speeches sound like democracy's promises.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Reframed the inaugural speeches of Putin, Maduro and Erdoğan as cautionary evidence, warning European voters in a pivotal election year that beautiful political rhetoric often precedes the dismantling of press freedom. Positioned RSF as the trusted counterweight to autocratic charm during its 30th anniversary.

visual.style_description: Stark black backgrounds with monospaced typewriter-style typography evoking redacted documents and journalistic transcripts, paired with grainy black-and-white photojournalism of persecuted reporters. Dense long-copy layouts mimic printed speeches, lending editorial gravitas.

idea.rationale: Juxtaposing autocrats' hopeful inaugural speeches with images of journalists they later jailed is a sharp, literate device — but it is not native to outdoor. The reveal lives in the long copy, which means it doesn't land in a glance from a public space; you have to read paragraphs of Putin's 2000 speech to feel the punch, which is a print-essay logic more than a billboard logic.

strategy.rationale: Reporters Without Borders (RSF) is the global press-freedom NGO; its German chapter, marking its 30th anniversary, exists to defend journalists and warn democracies about creeping authoritarianism — so the idea is tightly coherent with mission. The context (2024 as a global super-election year with 80+ countries voting) is a real, verifiable cultural fact, and the insight — that autocrats' first speeches sound democratic — is evidenced by using actual archived speeches of Putin, Maduro and Erdoğan rather than invented copy. What's missing is any sourced data on press-freedom decline (RSF publishes its own World Press Freedom Index — not leveraged on the board).

execution.rationale: Craft is high: monospaced typewriter typography, redacted-document aesthetic and grainy photojournalism give editorial gravitas appropriate to the subject. But the channel mix (long-copy posters, DOOH, magazine spreads, QR to donate) is conventional NGO-print orchestration with no clear phased rollout, partnership layer, or PR amplification engine described; for an Outdoor entry, the dense speech-transcript format fights the medium rather than exploiting public-space dynamics.

impact.rationale: The board reports no metrics whatsoever — no reach figures, donation lift, petition signatures, press pickup, or shifts in awareness around RSF Germany's 30th anniversary. The expected business outcome for an NGO appeal (donations via the QR code, or measurable salience ahead of the 2024 elections) is conspicuously absent, so impact can only be assumed, not credited.

why_it_won.verdict: A literate, well-crafted editorial idea that earned its shortlist on intellectual rigour and timeliness, but couldn't medal because the execution is essentially long-form print masquerading as outdoor, with no impact evidence to compensate.

visual.style_description: Cinematic, warmly-lit documentary-style photography capturing candid 'caught in the act' moments of people licking ketchup, paired with bold red Heinz branding and clean editorial typography. Layout mixes hero print images with social-vertical mockups and OOH proof shots.

idea.rationale: Turning the embarrassing private ritual of chasing the last drop into a 'caught-in-the-act' photo series is a charming, on-brand extension of Heinz's irrational-love platform, but it is the Nth iteration of that platform (Draw Ketchup, Ketchup Fraud, etc.) rather than a new creative leap. The licking visuals are memorable in a glance and native to print/OOH, yet the conceit feels familiar enough that jurors would see it as 'another Heinz love proof' rather than a fresh idea.

strategy.rationale: Heinz is a globally known condiment brand whose platform 'It Has to Be Heinz' celebrates irrational consumer devotion — the licking insight is fully coherent with that mission. The insight is presented as observed social behaviour ('based on a true licking story') rather than backed by a specific study or data point, so it rests on cultural truth and anecdote; it's believable but not rigorously sourced on the board.

execution.rationale: Craft is high — warmly-lit, cinematic documentary photography with clean editorial typography and disciplined red branding across mother/child, sleeve and dashboard scenarios. The orchestration is conventional Heinz playbook (print hero images + vertical social cutdowns + OOH posters) with no distinctive placement or amplification mechanic beyond standard PR seeding; the channels fit the audience but don't extend the idea.

impact.rationale: The board leans on 392M impressions in 15 days and a '310% average engagement rate' — but 310% of what baseline is undefined, and impressions are an entrant-reported reach claim with no sales, penetration or brand-tracking lift attached. Press logos (CNN, Vice, Fast Company) substantiate cultural pickup, yet for a category-leading FMCG the conspicuous absence of any business outcome (volume, share, household penetration) caps the impact case.

why_it_won.verdict: Shortlisted as a well-crafted, on-platform Heinz love-proof — but it didn't medal because it's a familiar variation on an idea Heinz has already mined repeatedly, with reach claims standing in for real business outcomes.

It's memorable and native to outdoor, but the mechanic (brand-impersonation poster homage) is a familiar streetwear/Netflix trick rather than a category-redefining device.

strategy.rationale: Netflix is a global SVOD platform whose mission here is to drive viewership for a tentpole French original; Lupin, the gentleman thief, is one of its strongest IPs and 'robbing' luxury houses is perfectly in-character. The Paris Fashion Week timing is a real, verifiable cultural peak that maximises eyeballs on luxury OOH — however, no specific insight, audience data, or sourced cultural stat is presented; the strategy rests on character logic rather than evidenced insight.

execution.rationale: Craft is the strongest pillar: the fashion-editorial photography, serif typography and crest-style emblems convincingly mimic luxury codes, and the kiosk placement near a Métropolitain entrance anchors the gag in authentic Parisian fashion territory. Orchestration, however, looks thin — a handful of poster executions in selected sites plus organic PR pickup, with no visible phasing, partnership layer, or social activation engineered to extend the heist beyond the kiosk.

impact.rationale: The board lists an impressive press roster (GQ, Le Figaro, Le Parisien, TF1 Info, Stratégies, Lürzer's Archive) suggesting genuine trade and mainstream pickup, but provides zero hard numbers — no impressions, no viewership lift for Lupin Part 3, no social metrics, no sentiment data. The obvious business outcome (Part 3 launch-week viewership) is conspicuously absent, leaving impact as an unquantified PR-coverage claim.

why_it_won.verdict: A beautifully crafted, in-character stunt that charmed the jury on idea and craft, but the absence of any quantified business or cultural result kept it from clearing the Bronze bar.

one_liner: The car check-up becomes the liver check-up.

extracted.insight: Men don't care about their health, but they care about their cars a lot.

TV commercial featuring a man with car/liver issues

inferred.one_liner: The car check-up becomes the liver check-up.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Reframed Poland's mandatory vehicle inspection into a men's health screening ritual, reaching a notoriously health-averse audience and driving tens of thousands of real liver tests. Shifted male health behaviour by piggybacking on a habit they already trusted: caring for their car.

visual.style_description: Split-composition poster pairing a mechanic and a doctor in mirrored poses, overlaid on a dark dashboard-gauge background with a liver-shaped warning icon. Cinematic photography with bold orange typography and tile-laid supporting stills.

idea.rationale: Piggybacking the mandatory car inspection — a ritual Polish men already submit to — onto a liver test is a clean, memorable behavioural hijack that aligns the insight (men care about cars, not bodies) with a tangible action. However, the poster itself (mirrored mechanic/doctor split with a liver gauge) reads as an explainer rather than a single-glance outdoor mnemonic; it needs the case context to fully land, which weakens it as pure Outdoor craft.

strategy.rationale: Essentiale is a Sanofi liver-support OTC brand whose commercial mission is to drive liver-health awareness and consultation — so a campaign that converts men into liver-test takers is tightly coherent with the brand's category role. The context is anchored in verifiable public-health data (Polish men's 8-year life-expectancy gap; liver failure implicated in over 50% of deaths) and the insight (Polish male car-obsession + mandatory yearly inspection) is a real cultural-legal truth, not a manufactured one. The strategic chain from problem → behavioural insight → ritual hijack is genuinely tight.

execution.rationale: Channel-to-target fit is the strongest execution element: testing inside the largest car-control-station chain reaches the exact health-averse male at the exact moment he is already accepting a 'check-up' mindset, and the orchestration layered TV, on-site dietitians, race-driver partnerships (Young Timer Warsaw, Truckers Life, Speedcar) and a digital hub to sustain reach. Craft of the board itself is competent but conventional healthcare-poster work — cinematic split composition, orange-on-black — and the campaign behaves more like an integrated/PR play than a true Outdoor execution, which likely capped jury enthusiasm in this category.

impact.rationale: 82,000 actual liver tests is the load-bearing number here and it is a real behavioural outcome (not a surrogate), directly tied to the brand's category objective — that anchors the case. The 205M impressions and 1,400 publications are entrant-reported reach claims without independent verification, and there is no disclosed shift in Essentiale sales, consideration, or in male screening rates beyond the program itself, so the cultural-footprint case is thinner than the headline numbers suggest.

why_it_won.verdict: A genuinely smart behavioural hijack with a real behaviour-change number (82K tests), but the board is a competent healthcare poster rather than an Outdoor-native idea — enough to clear the screening, not enough to convert the Outdoor jury into a medal.

The context ('every 45 minutes a man dies of prostate cancer in the UK') is a verifiable cultural-stat, and the insight that fear of rectal exam suppresses testing is a well-documented behavioural truth, though the board doesn't cite a specific study quantifying avoidance.

execution.rationale: Large-format OOH in London during Prostate Cancer Awareness Month (March 2024) is the right channel and right moment, with a QR code routing to information and donations as a basic conversion layer. Craft is high — editorial serif typography against crude photography is a deliberate, well-judged clash — but the orchestration is thin: a poster run plus organic social pickup, with no partnership, NHS tie-in, or sustained phasing visible.

impact.rationale: Claimed 3.5M reach and 44K social reactions in two weeks are modest surrogate metrics, and both are entrant-reported without third-party verification. The insight that fashion never shows the invisible majority Celio actually dresses is a real cultural truth, and Street View as a pre-existing archive of those men is a verifiable, witty proof. The board doesn't cite hard data on customer perception pre-campaign, which keeps it from being airtight.

execution.rationale: Craft is clean: the Street View UI is preserved as the layout system, giving the OOH instant recognisability, with price tags reinforcing it's a real product ad. Channel mix (OOH, DOOH, cinema, social) is coherent for a French mass retailer and the thumbnail-strip device travels well across formats, but the orchestration is fairly conventional paid-media rollout with no notable amplification layer or partnership beyond earned press.

impact.rationale: 39M reached is plausible for a national French OOH push but is a surrogate metric; +17% brand consideration is the meaningful shift, though the board doesn't disclose the base or methodology. 'Leading seller of jeans in France in 2023' is a strong business claim but causation to this campaign is asserted, not proven, and trade press pickup (Adweek, Drum, Stratégies) is industry-internal rather than cultural conversation.

why_it_won.verdict: A neat, on-brand platform-jailbreak that earned its shortlist on idea-to-brand fit, but the 'found footage as campaign' mechanic isn't fresh enough and the business results aren't cleanly attributed — so the jury stopped short of a Lion.
